records.title: Concept, Research, and Writing
records.summary: The public argument for Sutton SignWriting begins with language beyond sound, moves through literacy and reading, and then shows why signed-language writing can work as writing. Begin with the short orientation entry if you are new. Then choose the material that fits your question: foundations for the big idea, research for the developmental and reading model, or writing for how SignWriting works as writing.

records.title: DMS Plan
records.summary: A Data Management and Sharing Plan is only useful when it matches what the project will actually do. The plan needs policy language and a truthful account of the project lifecycle, data sources, standards, repository target, and responsibilities. Good DMS planning stays tied to execution.

Builder and review support for teams that need a structured starting point

records.title: Evidence and Adoption
records.summary: Documented use, adoption evidence, and current country anchors show how Sutton SignWriting claims can be evaluated. Start here when the question is empirical: what the current evidence base shows, what remains partial, where adoption is documented, and how claims should stay bounded.

records.title: Formal SignWriting
records.summary: Formal SignWriting is the technical model that lets Sutton SignWriting become sign text software can store, search, check, render, inspect, and process. The Shape of a Sign gives public readers the plainest introduction to the model. Technical readers can move directly into the Formal SignWriting series overview and opening papers. Both paths lead to the same architecture for readable and processable sign text.
keywords: The two-part word of time and space, with sequence and signbox kept distinct
FSW and SWU as two encodings of one model, with FSW usually canonical internally
Grammar, search, rendering, styling, and implementation as related but distinct layers
A clean entry point for developers, researchers, and standards-minded readers

Why query language, ranges, and variance make search a first-class technical consequence of the model.
SVG, fonts, layout, and output models kept distinct from the canonical text layer.
Alphabet overview, supporting context, the viewer, and the full base-symbol reference.
Category, group, base, fill, and rotation browsing for the ISWA 2010 alphabet.
Filtered table of the full 652-base ISWA 2010 inventory.

records.title: Iowa Collaboration
records.summary: Iowa conversations can begin with practical research data capacity now through Steve’s DMS Plan, workflow implementation, and repository delivery work, with longer-term SignWriting stewardship available as strategic context. The strongest first step is practical: help active labs finish work they already need done. From there, a PI, lab, or unit relationship can support work that is valuable now and strategically useful over time.

records.title: ISWA 2010
records.summary: The International SignWriting Alphabet 2010 is the shared symbol inventory for modern Sutton SignWriting fonts, editors, palettes, rendering, and formal encodings. ISWA 2010 is organized by category, group, base, fill, and rotation. These pages present the alphabet, the viewer, the full base-symbol table, and the principal historical references.

records.title: Repository Delivery
records.summary: Repository delivery is where planning, structure, documentation, validation, and technical labor have to converge into something submit-ready. Teams often need help getting data into the form a repository can actually accept and understand. For NDA and similar destinations, that can mean harmonization, dictionaries, file preparation, validation, uploads, issue resolution, audit support, recurring submissions, and maintenance as the project changes over time.
keywords: Repository-facing structure, dictionaries, validation, submission packages, and correction cycles

Assess what the repository requires and what the project currently has
Prepare or improve repository-facing files, dictionaries, documentation, and validation routines
Support submission, correction cycles, audit reporting, recurring updates, and large data movement
Keep delivery connected to the DMS Plan and workflow implementation so the project is not surprised late

records.title: Reviews and Critical Commentary
records.summary: The review papers evaluate scholarship, standards work, and recurring conceptual issues in signed-language writing. These papers ask whether the literature and standards discussion describe SignWriting accurately and at the right level. Readers can begin with an applied review paper and use the full-path critique framework when they need the shared method.

records.title: Stewardship and Institutional Coordination
records.summary: Sutton SignWriting has public technical, textual, and data infrastructure whose continuity depends on durable stewardship functions. Start here for the structures that can preserve, maintain, document, and responsibly extend existing SignWriting infrastructure. The materials gather host requirements, stewardship models, continuity risks, and institutional conversations.

records.title: Workflow Implementation
records.summary: The hard work often sits between the plan and the repository: source systems, exports, transformations, documentation, and validation. Operational data work rarely fits neatly into a policy document. Data may live across REDCap, spreadsheets, CSV exports, imaging outputs, outside systems, scripts, and collaborator files. Steve helps teams analyze that environment, name the actual workflow, build repeatable structure, and reduce the ambiguity that usually accumulates before delivery.

records.title: About ISWA 2010
records.summary: ISWA 2010 is the symbol inventory for modern Sutton SignWriting computing, organized as a stable alphabet rather than an open-ended drawing set. ISWA 2010 organizes the alphabet by category, group, base, fill, and rotation. It is the symbol layer used by fonts, editors, palettes, rendering, and Formal SignWriting encodings.
keywords: Categories divide the alphabet into major regions such as Hands, Movement, Head and Faces, Body, and Punctuation
Groups organize related symbol families within each category
Each base identifies one panel within the alphabet
Each panel is arranged as 6 fill values by 16 rotation values
ISWA 2010 defines one bounded inventory for compatible Sutton SignWriting work
The alphabet is used across fonts, palettes, websites, rendering tools, and formal encodings
Palm facing, fill, rotation, and related contrasts remain part of symbol identity
Compatible fonts redraw the alphabet without redefining symbol identity
Changes to the inventory produce forks rather than minor implementation variations
Formal SignWriting defines the structured sign model built from that layer
FSW and SWU are equivalent encodings of the formal model, not replacements for the alphabet
Rendering, search, and tooling all depend on stable symbol identity
Category: top-level division of the alphabet
Group: related family within a category
Panel: 6 by 16 matrix of related symbol forms for one base identity
Base: underlying symbol identity for a panel, referenced by a key such as S100
Fill: one axis of variation across the panel, with six values
Rotation: the second axis of variation across the panel, with sixteen values
Symbol key: complete FSW key combining base, fill, and rotation, such as S10000
Categories, groups, base symbols, and the fill-and-rotation matrix.
Filtered reference table for the full base-symbol inventory.

records.title: About Steve
records.summary: Steve Slevinski combines research data infrastructure, software development, standards work, and long-term stewardship of Sutton SignWriting technical resources. His current professional work helps research teams with DMS Plans, workflow implementation, and repository delivery. His long-term public work maintains and extends the technical foundations that make Sutton SignWriting usable in software, education, research, and public infrastructure.
keywords: Bachelor of Science in Mathematics from Grove City College with long-term work across software, structured data, workflow design, and technical systems

Started working with Valerie Sutton and SignWriting communities around the world in 2004

records.title: Licensing and Reuse
records.summary: Clear licenses for software, fonts, publications, and related research outputs. Those materials are meant to be used, studied, implemented, shared, and cited, but they do not all belong under the same license. Software is licensed as software. Fonts are licensed as fonts. Papers, briefs, guides, memos, slides, and companion documents are licensed as publications. The ISWA 2010 and Formal SignWriting Internet-Drafts, released through the IETF, also remain part of the public record and were released for reference, examination, implementation, evaluation, and unlimited distribution. If a package, font, or document includes its own more specific license statement, follow that specific statement.
keywords: Use email if you want clarification on reuse, citation, translation, or republication.
Background, current work, and the longer arc of the project.

solve series signed language writing critical review signed language writing critical review writing systems text infrastructure and critical evaluation the critical method and review series for signed language writing a full path framework and structured reviews that evaluate scholarship standards tools datasets and infrastructure claims at the layer they actually address and against the problem they actually claim to solve signed language writing critical review series overview full path review of signed language writing notation encoding standards tools datasets and infrastructure claims signed language writing critical review series a literature review series this series reviews scholarship standards and proposals about signed language writing it asks a direct question what does a source actually establish once writing notation encoding rendering and signed language structure are kept distinct the central review problem is signed language literacy as durable written text a source may be valuable as notation transcription semantic representation corpus annotation video linked documentation rendering standards work dataset infrastructure or ai tooling the review question is whether that value is being accurately located or whether it is being treated as if it solved the writing and literacy problem the series exists because this literature repeatedly runs into a small set of layer problems level confusion between language writing notation and encoding alphabetic bias in the evaluation of signed writing incomplete engagement with prior signed writing practice and technical tooling symbol centric analysis that misses the written sign as the primary unit full path overextension where a useful layer level result is treated as if it solved the whole signed text path data object collapse where written signs dictionary records corpus items media datasets rights and permissions are treated as the same object novelty claims that do not adequately engage existing work this is a critical review series in the ordinary scholarly sense sources are read carefully useful contributions are preserved stronger and weaker claims are separated conclusions are tested against evidence structure and actual practice the series applies a coherent evaluative lens shaped by sustained work on signed writing as durable text writing system adequacy written form structure text model adequacy and infrastructure viability that lens is offered as a tool for comparison not as a single global ranking of all signed language systems the lens emerged from recurring overextensions and unresolved layer mismatches that become especially visible once signed writing is treated as writing text and infrastructure rather than as notation alone for that reason the series is strongest when the question is full path adequacy for durable signed writing and less final when the question is a narrower niche such as transcription pedagogical bridge use semantic software video access or dataset construction author position and method the author of this series is also a developer and steward of formal signwriting and related sutton signwriting infrastructure that position gives the series unusually close knowledge of the technical stack symbol inventories written signs encodings fonts editors dictionaries corpora rendering paths search behavior migration problems public sites unicode facing work and stewardship requirements the method is therefore structured for inspection the framework is applied to sutton signwriting and formal signwriting before it is used only on external work review judgments distinguish layer level success from full path adequacy useful contributions from other systems are preserved even when their larger claims are narrowed standards success is separated from writing system completeness and production compatibility the constructive synthesis keeps alternative systems in view for transcription pedagogy handwriting semantic modeling data tooling and local practice the result is a declared standpoint with a method that readers can inspect challenge and reuse what this series reviews the series focuses on work that touches one or more of these areas signed language writing systems sutton signwriting notation and transcription literacy and biliteracy orthography and writing system theory sign language software and computational representation grammar encoding and text models for signed language technical standards and proposals where signed text is modeled throughout the series a few terms are used consistently sutton signwriting when the formal identity of valerie sutton s writing system needs to be explicit signwriting for ordinary references to that writing system formal signwriting for the technical text model that preserves sutton signwriting as structured text written sign for the primary written unit signbox for the spatial frame in which that unit is composed text model for the encoding layer that preserves written signs as text plane based writing for writing in which spatial composition is part of authorship durable signed text for signed text that persists across time and supports revisiting revision citation search teaching and software processing technical infrastructure for alphabets encodings fonts editors corpora datasets renderers tools and platforms human and institutional infrastructure for teachers communities of practice publishing habits stewardship funding policy and institutions adjacent purpose systems for notation transcription semantic representation corpus annotation rendering datasets or other systems that may be useful without satisfying full path writing infrastructure requirements this is reference terminology not proprietary terminology the terms are used because signwriting has made these distinctions concrete through writing encoding rendering search dictionary corpus standards and stewardship work other systems may use different terms but serious comparison requires mapping their terms to these distinctions or explaining why the distinctions do not apply the term pathways is also used more carefully across the project language pathways for routes of language access and expression literacy pathways for routes of reading and writing reading pathways for the narrower page to perception to interpretation problem during reading research method the reviews use a shared evaluative framework its governing principle is simple a system must be evaluated at the level it claims to operate on that principle is not a polite caveat it is the method a system that does not support readable writable revisitable signed text does not solve signed language literacy as written language even if it is strong and useful for another purpose in practice that means each review asks what the source claims to do what layer or adjacent purpose category it belongs to what primary unit and representational target it assumes how it handles space sequence text structure and data objects which layers it solves borrows receives from external practice or infrastructure assumes defers or leaves outside its scope what it accounts for in existing signed writing practice and infrastructure what its evidence maturity authority and prior work engagement support what layer level judgment and full path judgment follow readers who want the full methodological version can begin with full path critique framework for signed language writing and text infrastructure review procedure for the full path critique framework most readers do not need to start there series structure the series has five main parts in the complete pdf these parts are the main navigation structure method internal baseline core external reviews outer theoretical horizon and constructive synthesis method entries these define the review method and its main distinctions full path critique framework for signed language writing and text infrastructure internal baseline entries these apply the same standards inward so that the series states its own baseline rather than only judging outside work sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems these two pieces do different work the self application paper clarifies sutton signwriting and formal signwriting on their own terms the layer based paper demonstrates the method across a core four case comparison set core external review set these are the main external review targets in the series a review of official unicode signwriting a review of grushkin 2017 a review of filhol 2020 a review of thiessen 2011 a review of martin 2000 these entries are where the recurring critique categories become concrete outer theoretical horizon these entries widen the frame beyond the immediate signwriting debate and ask whether broader writing theory can actually accommodate signed writing a review of meletis and d rscheid 2022 the meletis and d rscheid entry also includes a companion paper signed writing as a pressure test for general writing theory that companion does a different job from the review itself it uses the review target as a lead case for the larger question of how far current grapholinguistics can actually stretch once signed writing is treated as a full comparative case constructive synthesis this entry turns the review series toward next step priorities rather than further diagnosis from critique to construction priorities for signed language writing infrastructure how to use this series there are several good entry paths depending on what you need best path for a new reader this overview entry a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems use this path if you want the shortest route into the series without beginning from the full framework fastest path into the method full path critique framework for signed language writing and text infrastructure a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems use this path if you want the shortest route from method to comparative demonstration best path for understanding the series baseline full path critique framework for signed language writing and text infrastructure sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework a review of official unicode signwriting use this path if you want the internal conceptual and technical baseline before moving into external reviews best path for core literature review a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems a review of grushkin 2017 a review of thiessen 2011 a review of filhol 2020 a review of martin 2000 use this path if you want to move from the comparative overview into the main external review cases best path for broader theory full path critique framework for signed language writing and text infrastructure a review of meletis and d rscheid 2022 signed writing as a pressure test for general writing theory use this path if your immediate question is how signed writing bears on general writing system theory best path for next steps a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems a review of official unicode signwriting from critique to construction priorities for signed language writing infrastructure use this path if your immediate question is what the field most needs next in research tooling standards and adoption relationship to other series sutton signwriting foundations asks why signed writing matters sutton signwriting language and literacy research asks what research and literacy questions follow writing and representation in sutton signwriting asks what kind of writing signwriting is formal signwriting asks how signed text works as a technical model signed language writing critical review series asks how the literature and standards work align with those foundations this series therefore stands beside the language and literacy writing systems and formal technical tracks rather than inside them in practice formal signwriting supplies the technical baseline for many of the reviews it shows what a complete text model for sutton signwriting must preserve before standards proposals or alternative systems can be compared fairly its role is evaluative to test outside work against clarified conceptual and technical distinctions to identify what remains useful and to make recurring misunderstandings easier to recognize recurring critique categories across the entries recurring critique patterns are named and reused so that the reviews can be compared without flattening their differences level confusion full path overextension symbol centric modeling forced linearization character inventory substitution renderer substitution alphabetic bias incomplete infrastructure engagement evidence overextension authority conflation incomplete prior work engagement data object collapse these categories are not substitutes for argument they are a way to make the review program cumulative and comparable across entries direction together these entries provide a typology of recurring issues in signed language writing scholarship a public evaluation layer for external literature and standards work a bridge from critique toward clearer theory and better design a basis for later survey papers response articles and technical commentaries a place where each review stands on its own as a readable paper while the framework remains available as the deeper shared method the aim is not only to identify problems it is to make future work clearer more precise and easier to build on

records.title: Sutton SignWriting Foundations

The conceptual foundation for Sutton SignWriting: language is not reducible to sound, writing is not inherently linear, and signed languages need durable read-write pathways of their own.
Plain-language orientation to Sutton SignWriting, the Sutton SignWriting Foundations series, and the core public argument.
records.contentExcerpt: Start Here What Sutton SignWriting is, why it matters, and what to read first Sutton SignWriting is a writing system for signed languages. This work is about making signed language usable as real text, not only as video, gloss, or research transcription. That matters because sign...
records.searchText: sutton signwriting foundations language literacy writing and the core conceptual argument for signwriting the conceptual foundation for sutton signwriting language is not reducible to sound writing is not inherently linear and signed languages need durable read write pathways of their own series foundations foundations language literacy writing and the core conceptual argument for signwriting the conceptual foundation for sutton signwriting language is not reducible to sound writing is not inherently linear and signed languages need durable read write pathways of their own start here plain language orientation to sutton signwriting the sutton signwriting foundations series and the core public argument start here what sutton signwriting is why it matters and what to read first sutton signwriting is a writing system for signed languages this work is about making signed language usable as real text not only as video gloss or research transcription that matters because signed languages are full human languages but they have often lacked one important layer durable readable and writable text within the language itself when a language has everyday writing people can do ordinary textual work with it they can jot something down revise a sentence compare two versions cite a passage search a collection annotate a lesson or build a reusable archive for many signed language communities those ordinary text practices have had to depend on tools that do not write the signed language directly video is powerful for recording signed language gloss is useful for cross language reference research transcription can help specialists analyze language but those tools do not do the same job as ordinary writing they do not give signers the same kind of direct written pathway that print gives many spoken languages or that braille gives blind readers signwriting matters because it offers one serious answer to that missing layer it can represent handshape movement facial marking and spatial structure in a form that can be read written revised taught cited and through compatible technical infrastructure searched and built into tools that does not make it a replacement for bilingual literacy video or community practice it makes it a written pathway for signed language itself two words recur across this series durable means that text can be revisited revised searched cited taught and reused infrastructure means the tools standards teaching institutions and stewardship that let writing persist the core idea the sutton signwriting foundations series makes two linked arguments first language is not sound human language can develop through different embodied pathways spoken language uses sound signed language uses vision and movement tactile language access matters too the point is not that modality is irrelevant the point is that no single sensory channel owns language second writing is not only a line many writing systems are line based but writing can also use space in other ways signwriting is important because it writes signed language through spatial composition rather than forcing it into a line first together those claims make room for signed language literacy on its own terms the first claim establishes that signed languages do not need sound in order to count as full language the second establishes that writing for signed languages does not need to imitate the line in order to count as full writing what to read there are three entries in this public sutton signwriting foundations series start here this orientation gives the simplest overview and names the main argument language without sound this essay explains why language is not reducible to speech and why signed languages need durable readable and writable pathways of their own the shape of writing this essay explains why writing is not inherently linear and why signwriting should be understood as plane based writing read those three in order if you are new to the project the larger platform the rest of the sutton signwriting platform carries the deeper specialist work sutton signwriting language and literacy research develops the research facing pathway model writing and representation in sutton signwriting expands the writing systems argument sutton signwriting evidence and adoption documents use feasibility and evidence boundaries sutton signwriting infrastructure and program explains stewardship and long term continuity formal signwriting documents the technical text model signed language writing critical review series evaluates literature standards proposals and recurring claims those series are not required before reading sutton signwriting foundations they are where the questions go next documentation path this foundations series gives the conceptual entry point the rest of the platform carries the detailed documentation language development literacy pathways and reading questions are developed in sutton signwriting language and literacy research public use evidence adoption limits and country level anchors are documented in sutton signwriting evidence and adoption plane based writing authored space and writing system theory are developed in writing and representation in sutton signwriting search encoding symbol identity validation layout rendering and software processing are documented in formal signwriting and unicode and signwriting continuity stewardship institutional support and program design are developed in sutton signwriting infrastructure and program why this matters durable text changes what people can do with language it can be revisited revised cited searched compared taught annotated processed and built into corpora and datasets for signed languages that is not only a technical question it is a literacy question a research question an educational question and an infrastructure question sutton signwriting foundations begins with the simplest claim signed languages are full human languages and they deserve durable written pathways of their own sutton signwriting is one serious answer to that need

authored space iconicity convention legibility handwriting machine encoding kinematography and broader typological questions writing and representation in sutton signwriting overview plane based composition authored space convention handwriting machine encoding and typology define the writing systems question for signwriting writing and representation in sutton signwriting a writing systems series this series is the writing systems part of the sutton signwriting platform its purpose is to define the conceptual place of signwriting as writing plane based authored conventional material encodable and typologically consequential it is for writing systems scholars grapholinguists scholars of notation script and representation readers who want to understand how signwriting works as writing the central questions of the series include why writing is not inherently linear how signwriting uses plane based composition where signwriting fits in writing system typology how authored perspective and spatial relation function in writing how iconicity becomes disciplined through convention and legibility how handwriting and material practice shape spatial script culture how human writing differs from machine encoding whether signed writing exposes a missing representational type in writing system typology how signwriting differs from other conceptions of signed writing how these conceptual questions connect to the formal signbox model series shape this series has six conceptual anchors one companion bridge note one comparative support entry and two bridge readings from adjacent series together they give the series a typological center the conceptual anchors are plane based writing and script typology perspective and authored space iconicity convention and legibility handwriting and spatial script practice human writing and machine encoding kinematography and the limits of writing system typology the comparative support entry is other sign language scripts and their conceptions the companion bridge note is point line and plane evidence notes the two most important bridge readings are the shape of writing in sutton signwriting foundations the shape of a sign in formal signwriting terms map this series uses several terms that belong to different axes and layers of the argument the following map is meant to keep those terms from collapsing into one another term axis or layer meaning in this series plane based writing organizational writing in which the primary written unit is composed through authored spatial relation within a two dimensional field kinematography representational a candidate analytic term and pressure test not settled field terminology for writing whose primary representational work is articulatory spatial and kinetic signbox written unit field the bounded plane where selected signwriting symbols are composed into one written sign authored space authorship the writer selected placement relation viewpoint and composition inside the written form iconicity visual motivation motivated visual relation between written form and signed form not by itself sufficient for writing convention writing system discipline shared constraints that make visual forms repeatable teachable correctable and reusable use based convention writing system practice shared written forms emerge through writer choice reader acceptance teaching practice dictionary use corpus frequency and repeated reuse rather than central prescription legibility reader practice recognition within a learned system of written contrasts not mere visual transparency handwriting material practice human production of the script through block printing shorthand cursive forms drafts correction and ordinary use encoding technical preservation machine readable preservation of the written form rendering presentation visible output produced from encoded text through fonts svg layout engines or related display systems reading order the clearest short path through this series is the shape of writing plane based writing and script typology optional support point line and plane evidence notes perspective and authored space iconicity convention and legibility handwriting and spatial script practice human writing and machine encoding kinematography and the limits of writing system typology the shape of a sign if you want the comparative support layer after that read other sign language scripts and their conceptions entry roles the shape of writing inherited conceptual anchor for the series argues that writing is not inherently linear introduces the point line and plane frame plane based writing and script typology typological anchor of this series argues that signwriting needs both a representational axis and an organizational axis defines plane based writing as an analytic descriptor situates the signbox as a writing systems issue before it becomes a technical model point line and plane evidence notes companion bridge note rather than a separate main paper supports the move from the shape of writing to the writing systems series identifies the evidence and infrastructure questions behind the point line plane frame perspective and authored space writing systems anchor of this series develops viewpoint authored relation and composed space prepares the bridge to the signbox iconicity convention and legibility writing systems anchor of this series explains how motivated visual form becomes disciplined writing distinguishes iconic writing from free illustration prepares the bridge from authored space to technical preservation handwriting and spatial script practice writing systems anchor of this series treats writing as human material practice rather than only formal model or rendered output names block printing shorthand cursive forms and ordinary script culture as writing systems questions prepares the bridge from convention and legibility to technical encoding human writing and machine encoding writing systems anchor of this series separates human written signs from machine encodings explains why serialization is not the same as linearization prepares the bridge to formal signwriting without turning the writing systems argument into a technical specification kinematography and the limits of writing system typology capstone of this series develops kinematography as a candidate representational axis term distinguishes plane based organization from articulatory spatial kinetic representation keeps the term bounded as a pressure test rather than settled terminology connects this series to the grapholinguistic pressure test work in the critical review series the shape of a sign technical bridge in formal signwriting formalizes the signbox symbols coordinates and bounded written object other sign language scripts and their conceptions comparative support entry distinguishes alphabetic teaching devices analytic notation and plane based script conceptions keeps the signwriting argument aware of neighboring work without making those neighboring systems the center of the series relationship to adjacent series sutton signwriting foundations opens the public conceptual door writing and representation in sutton signwriting deepens the writing systems argument formal signwriting provides the mechanism layer that follows from these concepts sutton signwriting evidence and adoption supports this series by showing where learned signed writing is already visible in practice signed language writing critical review series tests the broader writing theory and standards claims especially through the meletis and d rscheid review and companion paper in that series kinematography is used diagnostically here it is developed A bounded four-case comparison showing how layer-level and full-path judgments clarify signed language writing, encoding, and standards claims. A comparative demonstration across Filhol, Grushkin, Thiessen, and Unicode SignWriting
records.parentTitle: A Layer-Based Evaluation of Signed Language Writing Systems
keywords: Signed Language Writing Critical Review Series
A Layer-Based Evaluation of Signed Language Writing Systems
