They do make one recurring pattern visible. Each source identifies a real part of the s...
records.searchText: a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems paper paper for a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems a bounded four case comparison showing how layer level and full path judgments clarify signed language writing encoding and standards claims a comparative demonstration across filhol grushkin thiessen and unicode signwriting series signed language writing critical review a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems primary signed language writing critical review series a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems paper comparative paper working paper a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems a comparative demonstration across filhol grushkin thiessen and unicode signwriting these four cases do not make the whole field they do make one recurring pattern visible each source identifies a real part of the signed writing problem but each also shifts the main problem onto a different layer filhol 2020 where semantic representation and software workflow pressures are central grushkin 2017 where literacy and orthographic prescription are central thiessen 2011 where signwriting structure is taken seriously but analyzed too low unicode signwriting where standardization succeeds at one layer while remaining incomplete at another taken together they show how good insights can still produce incomplete conclusions when writing encoding semantic representation literacy design and symbol analysis are not kept distinct enough this paper is a bounded core comparison not a total survey of the field it can still serve as an orientation piece because a small bounded comparison shows the method quickly it should not be mistaken for the whole review set introduction work on signed language writing systems notation representation and encoding often identifies real problems while still arriving at incomplete conclusions that pattern is not random it usually arises because a real insight is placed at the wrong level sometimes the writing problem is replaced by a semantic problem sometimes by a literacy transfer problem sometimes by a symbol classification problem sometimes by a character standardization problem all four selected works identify genuine pressures software and queryability the need for writing signwriting structure standardization and interoperability the comparison asks stricter questions what layer or adjacent purpose category the work actually occupies what primary unit and representational target it centers how it handles space sequence text structure and infrastructure which layers it solves borrows receives from external practice or infrastructure assumes defers or leaves outside its scope what evidence and authority support its claims where the layer level judgment differs from the full path judgment the companion matrix offers the compact version of the same argument method the governing principle is the same one stated in the framework entry a system must be evaluated at the level it claims to operate on applied to signed language writing it requires distinguishing at least the following language writing system notation transcription semantic representation encoding text model rendering for each case the comparison asks what primary layer or adjacent purpose category the work addresses whether it keeps its layers distinct what representational target it privileges what primary unit it centers how it handles spatial and sequential organization what sort of text model or technical model it gives which layers are solved borrowed externally supplied assumed deferred or not in scope how well it is grounded in actual sutton signwriting practice and infrastructure what evidence maturity authority and prior work engagement support its claims where the layer level judgment and full path judgment differ why these four cases each case highlights a different recurrent displacement filhol 2020 this is the semantic and software case it shows what happens when real software requirements are allowed to collapse into a writing system argument grushkin 2017 this is the literacy and orthography case it shows what happens when a strong necessity of writing argument is followed by an alphabetically biased design recommendation thiessen 2011 this is the structure and formalization case it shows what happens when signwriting is taken seriously and still analyzed one level too low through symbols rather than written signs unicode signwriting this is the standardization case it shows what happens when a real character level success is later treated as though it completed the entire writing problem together the four cases produce a compact but still differentiated pattern results filhol 2020 filhol identifies real needs editable signed language representation queryable signed language representation software integrated signed language representation synthesizable structure connected to avatar pipelines he is also right that spontaneous user diagrams tend toward plane based organization visually meaningful grouping forms that users can read more naturally than raw formal code those are real contributions the problem begins when those insights are extended into a writing system comparison without separating layer level success from full path adequacy filhol s strongest constructive proposal is really at the semantic plus software representation layer azee was designed to support human readable machine tractable graphical interfaces to formal meaning bearing structures that is legitimate and potentially useful but once this is treated as though it were also a writing system replacement argument several problems appear signwriting is treated too narrowly as chiefly phonographic already existing writable and queryable signwriting text is not engaged strongly enough novelty claims are made around properties such as plane based recursive structure without sufficient comparison to existing signed writing practice so filhol s paper is not best read as a successful reclassification of signed writing it is better read as a prompt toward semantic layer tooling graphical interfaces and software representation in declared dependency terms the semantic and software layers are the solved or directly addressed layers human writing written convention public text operations pedagogy and stewardship are not supplied by the proposal itself they become assumed layers only when the paper is read as a writing system replacement argument layer level judgment useful but narrow as semantic software and synthesis oriented representation full path judgment misleading when overextended into a writing system replacement argument because the proposal does not by itself supply human written units written convention durable text operations public use and stewardship grushkin 2017 grushkin s article is strongest in its first major claim signed languages benefit from writing that part of the article remains valuable grushkin correctly argues that gloss is inadequate as writing video is not a substitute for routine text written signed language could support pedagogy preservation communication and internal discourse these are substantial strengths and the review series should preserve them the problem appears when grushkin moves from signed languages need writing to signed languages especially in alphabetic majority societies are best served by alphabetic writing that second claim is much weaker than the first the article s comparative reasoning is governed too heavily by alphabetic expectations compatibility with english biliteracy phonological transfer assumptions comparison of signed scripts to alphabetic rather than signed language adequacy this leads grushkin to treat signwriting and related systems too shallowly as iconographic and to undervalue their internal structure as writing systems so grushkin s article is not best classified simply as misleading across the board it is better described more carefully strong on why signed languages need writing less supported and shaped by alphabetic bias on what kind of writing follows the declared dependencies are therefore uneven grushkin solves a conceptual and advocacy layer by showing why signed languages need writing the alphabetic recommendation assumes that transfer into english can govern orthographic design and leaves the signed written unit spatial composition text model infrastructure and comparative evidence unresolved layer level judgment correct and useful as a necessity of writing argument for signed languages full path judgment not full path adequate as an orthographic prescription because the alphabetic recommendation does not account for signed written units plane based composition text modeling or existing signwriting infrastructure thiessen 2011 thiessen provides the strongest early engagement with signwriting among the four cases that matters unlike grushkin he does not mainly evaluate signwriting from the outside through orthographic preference unlike filhol he does not mainly approach it through software or semantic representation he actually tries to describe how the system works he correctly recognizes the signbox spatial composition non arbitrary written structure the need for a one dimensional encoding for software use he also does serious script internal descriptive work around symbol classes fills rotations handedness viewpoints movement structure placement rules the problem is not superficial misunderstanding the problem is where he places the top level abstraction thiessen s main route into the problem is symbol first the thesis proceeds through symbol categories and only later reaches whole sign structure and encoding even where chapter 10 begins to move toward underlying sign structure tree relations attachment points and relative positioning this move remains late and explicitly preliminary so thiessen s limitation is not that he misses structure it is that he mislocates the strongest explanatory weight too low in symbol inventory in symbol combination in a language like grammar of signwriting rather than centering the written sign as the primary written unit from the start this makes thiessen a useful case of deferred rather than ignored dependency the thesis solves much of the script internal descriptive layer and directly recognizes the need for encoding but the written sign text model remains late and preliminary the missing full path layer is not invisible it is not yet governing layer level judgment correct and useful as script internal descriptive and computational work full path judgment structurally incomplete where the analysis treats symbol categories as the main abstraction instead of centering the written sign and authored spatial composition as the primary text structure unicode signwriting the unicode case is different from the other three because it is not mainly a literature paper it is a standards case that means the evaluative standard must stay very clear unicode signwriting correctly recognized that signwriting is a real script that a character repertoire needed stable standardization that spatial composition is essential to the script those are real achievements but the standardization outcome was only partial at the character layer the result was meaningful at the written sign layer it remained incomplete that distinction is the whole review unicode signwriting helps solve character naming public referenceability part of the interoperability problem it does not complete a compatible written sign text model direct preservation of full writer selected symbol state across the system clean migration from current sutton signwriting production ecosystems spatial sign encoding in a way that resolves the written sign problem so the unicode case is not an instance of total level confusion it is an instance of correct multi layer awareness partial completion and later over reading by others who treated character encoding as if it had solved writing as text the declared dependency pattern is especially clear here character repertoire and script recognition are solved layers existing signwriting practice and infrastructure are externally supplied layers the standard attempts to serve the written sign text model stable symbol identity sorting migration and production compatibility remain deferred or assumed layers when the standard is interpreted as a complete production replacement layer level judgment correct and useful as character repertoire standardization and script recognition work full path judgment structurally incomplete as a compatible production replacement because the written sign text model stable writer selected symbol identity spatial composition sorting migration and dataset compatibility remain unresolved cross case analysis the strongest comparative pattern is not that every case makes exactly the same mistake the stronger and more accurate pattern is this the writing problem is repeatedly displaced in each case one real layer of the problem becomes a stand in for the whole filhol foregrounds semantic structure and software representation grushkin foregrounds literacy transfer and alphabetic compatibility thiessen foregrounds symbol structure and rule governed combination unicode foregrounds character inventory and standardization each of those is a real part of the larger field none of them is the whole writing problem where a writing system or text model answer is actually needed the field repeatedly struggles to stabilize the right top level unit and relation set the written sign as the primary written unit authored spatial composition as part of the text and a clean distinction among writing encoding and rendering this is why the comparison matters it shows that the field s recurring problems are not merely differences of opinion about conclusions they are recurrent displacements of the problem itself scope of this paper this paper does claim that the four selected cases still form a strong core demonstration set that the layer based method works across multiple kinds of signed writing problem that a recurring comparative pattern can be shown across those cases the comparison is intentionally bounded these four cases do not exhaust the field replace the fuller series or reduce every later review to the same pattern they show why the layer based method is useful enough to carry forward conclusion the primary recurring problem in signed language writing research is not lack of insight it is repeated displacement of insight filhol identifies a real semantic software problem grushkin identifies a real writing need problem thiessen identifies a real structural problem unicode identifies a real character standardization problem but in each case the identified layer is pressed too far toward becoming the whole answer a more adequate field wide model requires recognizing the written sign as the primary written unit preserving spatial composition as authored text rather than decoration separating writing encoding and rendering cleanly and evaluating signed writing first on its own structural terms not only by its compatibility with surrounding spoken language norms that is the lasting value of the layer based method it does not merely produce separate reviews it makes the recurrent pattern behind them comparable closing statement the question is not whether signed language can be written it already is the question is whether the field is describing that writing at the right level

records.title: A Review of Filhol (2020) Review
records.summary: Review for A Review of Filhol (2020). Filhol is useful as semantic and software representation, but misleading when overextended into a writing-system replacement claim. A Human-Editable Sign Language Representation and the limits of representation as writing
keywords: Signed Language Writing Critical Review Series
records.contentExcerpt: A Review of Filhol (2020) A Human-Editable Sign Language Representation and the limits of representation as writing Reviewed source: Michael Filhol, "A Human-Editable Sign Language Representation Inspired by Spontaneous Productions . . . and a Writing System?" Sign Language Studi...
records.searchText: a review of filhol 2020 review review for a review of filhol 2020 filhol is useful as semantic and software representation but misleading when overextended into a writing system replacement claim a human editable sign language representation and the limits of representation as writing series signed language writing critical review filhol 2020 a human editable sign language representation primary signed language writing critical review series a review of filhol 2020 review literature review essay working review filhol 2020 a human editable sign language representation a review of filhol 2020 a human editable sign language representation and the limits of representation as writing reviewed source michael filhol a human editable sign language representation inspired by spontaneous productions and a writing system sign language studies 21 1 98 136 year 2020 doi 10 1353 sls 2020 0030 michael filhol s 2020 paper identifies real pressures in signed language representation video is weak for routine text use signed language needs editable and queryable structure software native representation matters those pressures are real the argument weakens when it turns those pressures into a writing system argument and treats signwriting too narrowly in the process executive summary framework judgment field judgment layer level judgment useful but narrow as semantic software and synthesis oriented representation full path judgment misleading when overextended into a writing system replacement argument because the proposal does not by itself supply human written units written convention durable text operations public use and stewardship main layer risk semantic and software representation are treated as though they answer the writing system and infrastructure problem refinement useful at the semantic software layer overstated when treated as a writing system replacement argument filhol argues that signed language needs a representation that is editable queryable synthesizable human readable he correctly observes that video is weak for the kinds of textual manipulation and software integration that signed language needs he then treats major signed writing systems including signwriting as essentially phonographic and argues that spontaneous user diagrams point instead toward a more meaning oriented plane based and partially logographic representation the result is best read as a useful semantic and software proposal combined with an overstated writing system reclassification what filhol sees clearly video is not writing this is one of the paper s most durable claims video is weak for ordinary editing searching routine textual manipulation software native textual workflows that insight holds queryability and computation matter the paper is also right to insist that signed language needs representations that can support structured querying software integration synthesis pipelines machine facing manipulation beyond media playback two dimensional composition matters its attention to spontaneous diagrammatic behavior and plane based composition is also useful here the paper comes closest to a real insight about signed writing where the argument reaches too far the main problem is not the pressures filhol identifies it is the level at which he places them together with the paper s limited accounting of what signwriting had already addressed signwriting is misclassified as merely phonographic the paper treats signwriting too narrowly as a form encoding system that does not adequately account for signwriting as plane based writing spatial composition as part of authorship the existence of structured sign text actual technical support for editing search and processing this is therefore a case of both level confusion and incomplete infrastructure engagement already solved technical problems are treated as if they remain unsolved the paper presents as open problems several issues that signwriting technical infrastructure has already addressed in meaningful form including structured textual representation software native sign text queryability computational processing that does not empty the paper of value it means the novelty claims need narrowing the constructive proposal belongs more to semantic representation than to signed writing system replacement the strongest constructive part of the paper is not a new account of signed writing as such it is a semantic layer proposal tied to azee and synthesis oriented workflows that is useful it is also a real niche a field that needs durable public writing may still need semantic formalisms for synthesis avatar work and machine facing representation those should not be treated as fake contributions merely because they do not by themselves yield a full public writing path it is simply not the same thing as proving that signwriting is the wrong paradigm for signed writing what the paper actually establishes the paper does not establish that signwriting is structurally inadequate that signed writing should move away from signwriting s core design that a meaning based diagrammatic representation should replace signwriting as writing what it does show is semantic and diagrammatic representations deserve serious attention software native manipulation of signed language is a real requirement video is insufficient as a substitute for text that is a meaningful contribution it is just a narrower one than the paper sometimes suggests review through the critique framework layer identification the paper moves across writing system questions semantic representation software processing it does not keep those levels distinct enough declared dependencies clarify the judgment the paper directly addresses semantic representation software manipulation and synthesis oriented workflows public writing written convention durable text operations pedagogy and stewardship are either outside the paper s strongest contribution or assumed when the argument is read as a writing system replacement claim read narrowly that is not a failure read as a full path writing claim those assumed layers become the problem representation model its most useful ideas point toward semantic representation its strongest missteps occur when those semantic layer concerns are used to reclassify signwriting as if they were writing system conclusions structural unit analysis the paper pays attention to spatial composition which is useful but it does not adequately recognize signwriting s written sign structure as already meaningful at the text level text model and infrastructure this is where the paper is weakest it does not sufficiently account for existing signwriting technical infrastructure standards and processable text forms main critique categories incomplete infrastructure engagement level confusion incomplete prior work engagement full path overextension final assessment this paper is worth reading because it identifies real pressures the insufficiency of video the importance of queryability the need for software native signed language representation it becomes overextended when it treats signwriting as merely phonographic and ignores the extent to which signwriting technical infrastructure has already realized parts of the design space it describes its lasting value is therefore not as a successful replacement argument for signed writing its lasting value is as a prompt toward semantic layer tooling and representation

records.title: A Review of Grushkin (2017) Review
records.summary: Review for A Review of Grushkin (2017). Grushkin is correct and useful on the need for signed writing, but not full-path adequate as an alphabetic prescription. Writing Signed Languages: What For? What Form? and the question of signed-language writing purpose
keywords: Signed Language Writing Critical Review Series
records.contentExcerpt: A Review of Grushkin (2017) Writing Signed Languages: What For? What Form? and the question of signed-language writing purpose Reviewed source: Donald A. Grushkin, "Writing Signed Languages: What For? What Form?" American Annals of the Deaf, 161(5), 509-527 Year: 2017 DOI: 10.135...
records.searchText: a review of grushkin 2017 review review for a review of grushkin 2017 grushkin is correct and useful on the need for signed writing but not full path adequate as an alphabetic prescription writing signed languages what for what form and the question of signed language writing purpose series signed language writing critical review grushkin 2017 writing signed languages what for what form primary signed language writing critical review series a review of grushkin 2017 review literature review essay working review grushkin 2017 writing signed languages what for what form a review of grushkin 2017 writing signed languages what for what form and the question of signed language writing purpose reviewed source donald a grushkin writing signed languages what for what form american annals of the deaf 161 5 509 527 year 2017 doi 10 1353 aad 2017 0001 donald a grushkin s 2017 article asks an important question what is signed writing for and what form should it take the article should be read in its educational and practitioner facing context it is trying to answer institutional questions about deaf education and biliteracy not only to produce a detached writing systems typology its lasting value comes from taking the need for signed writing seriously grushkin does not argue that signed languages merely could be written in some abstract sense he argues that they should be written that objections to signed writing are weak and that writing would materially benefit signed language communities in pedagogy preservation communication and literacy that is the article s central contribution its weakness appears later when it moves from the general need for signed writing to a specific orthographic prescription grushkin concludes that alphabetic signed writing is the best option especially where asl english biliteracy is a central goal that recommendation is not supported as strongly as the preceding necessity of writing argument so the article is strongest on why signed languages need writing and less well supported on what kind of writing follows framework judgment field judgment layer level judgment correct and useful as a necessity of writing argument for signed languages full path judgment not full path adequate as an orthographic prescription because the alphabetic recommendation does not account for signed written units plane based composition text modeling or existing signwriting infrastructure main layer risk alphabetic bias and full path overextension from a literacy need into an orthographic prescription refinement strong on why signed languages need writing weak on what kind of writing follows what the article sets out to do grushkin frames the article around a set of familiar objections to written signed language and a set of hoped for benefits he begins by noting that signed language communities are often treated and often treat themselves as fundamentally oral communities without writing he then argues that this should not be accepted as natural or final his project has two parts to advocate the development and dissemination of a written form of signed languages to argue in favor of an alphabetically based system over other possible orthographic formats the second goal matters because it shapes the whole article this is not only an argument for writing as such it is an argument for a particular kind of written signed language that context makes the english biliteracy concern understandable but it does not make it sufficient as the governing criterion for signed writing the article then moves through four main stages objections to written signed languages benefits of developing and accepting them literacy and biliteracy considerations comparative reasoning about orthographic form this overall structure is coherent the first half builds a broad pro writing case the second half narrows too quickly to an alphabetic recommendation what grushkin establishes well it argues convincingly that signed languages benefit from writing this is the article s strongest and most durable contribution grushkin argues that signed languages benefit from writing for literacy development pedagogy preservation internal community communication discussion of signed language in signed language the creation and storage of literature history and cultural record these arguments are not trivial they directly answer a persistent background assumption in signed language discourse that video is enough or that signed languages do not need writing in the same way spoken languages do grushkin is right to reject that assumption he understands that writing is not merely a way to archive language mechanically it is a social and cognitive technology it supports skimming scanning searching easy rereading routine text production communication across time and distance those are writing specific affordances video can preserve signed performance more exactly but it does not simply replace writing as a practical textual medium that point remains one of the strongest things in the article it is right that gloss is inadequate grushkin s critique of gloss is strong and should be preserved he points out that glossing depends on another language encourages false one to one mappings between sign and spoken language word obscures grammatical differences between signed and spoken languages makes signed languages appear incomplete or ungrammatical keeps signed language trapped inside the written form of the majority language this is exactly right gloss is not a writing system it is a research and instructional convenience it may be useful in narrow contexts but it does not provide a native written pathway for signed languages grushkin is also right to say that glossing reinforces misconceptions it can make asl look like reduced or broken english when what is really being seen is an english based representation of a structurally different language that critique remains fully usable it is right that video is not a substitute for writing grushkin correctly distinguishes between recording a performance and having writable text video is valuable it may indeed preserve more of an actual signed utterance than writing can but that is not the only criterion that matters writing supports ordinary textual practices that video does not support nearly as well quick browsing selective rereading easy quotation direct comparison annotation searching within documents economical routine exchange this argument is especially important because use video instead is one of the most common moves used to dismiss signed writing grushkin sees clearly that video and writing are not interchangeable media it sees real community and educational reasons for wanting signed writing the article is not limited to abstract writing system theory it also identifies community uses for written signed language that are easy to underestimate discussing signs directly without resorting to gloss or long prose descriptions writing in the community s preferred language preserving deaf literature and history supporting explicit asl instruction creating a more stable basis for deaf bilingual education even where one may disagree with the article s later orthographic recommendation these motivations are real grushkin is not solving a fake problem it correctly distinguishes notation from writing this is another good feature of the article grushkin distinguishes systems meant for linguistic transcription from systems meant for ordinary use he notes that notation attempts to capture fine detail for analysis whereas writing needs only enough structure for ordinary comprehension and routine manipulation that distinction matters because it blocks a common confusion in the literature the question is not merely whether sign languages can be transcribed of course they can the question is what kind of writing system is suitable for everyday textual use grushkin asks that question directly and that makes the article worth reviewing seriously where the argument narrows too quickly the article s limit appears when it turns from the need for signed writing to the recommendation of alphabetic signed writing signwriting and related systems are treated too shallowly as iconographic grushkin groups signwriting aslwrite and related visually organized systems under the label iconographic he uses this category to oppose them to alphabetic systems and to argue that alphabetic signed writing is better suited for biliteracy with english this is too shallow as a description of signwriting it is also too shallow as a description of the wider group even where one remains skeptical of their long term infrastructure adequacy some community oriented spatial systems have been real attempts to keep signed writing compact writable and stylistically alive in ways that cannot be dismissed as mere pictoriality the problem is not that signwriting contains iconic elements it obviously does the problem is that the article treats iconographic as though it were a sufficiently explanatory classification by itself that flattens several important distinctions iconicity versus lack of structure plane based spatial writing versus pictorial approximation a stable alphabet of written units versus free form drawing authored spatial relation as written form versus ornamental layout in other words the article tends to treat signwriting as though its visuality were its primary analytical fact that is not enough a serious account of signwriting has to reckon with it as a structured writing system with a stable inventory regular composition and authored spatial relations by reducing the comparison to alphabetic versus iconographic the article makes the competing systems easier to dismiss than they should be the evaluation standard is alphabetic from the start this is the article s main interpretive problem grushkin repeatedly evaluates signed writing by asking how readily it aligns with english style orthographic expectations and english biliteracy strategies this makes resemblance to alphabetic writing appear as a major virtue before the comparison has even been properly argued that is not a neutral comparison it is a case of alphabetic bias the article should have asked what kind of writing best fits signed language on its own terms instead it increasingly asks what kind of signed writing best aligns with the literacy needs created by an alphabetic majority language that is a different question it may be a practically important question but it cannot simply replace the first one once it does the inquiry shifts from evaluating writing systems as writing systems to evaluating them by compatibility with a surrounding spoken language norm the biliteracy argument does not justify the orthographic recommendation as strongly as claimed grushkin devotes substantial effort to literacy and biliteracy research he reviews work on phonological awareness deep and shallow orthography cross language transfer alphabetic and logographic reading strategies interference between writing systems this gives the article an appearance of strong comparative grounding but the actual conclusion drawn from this literature is stronger than the evidence warrants the article establishes a plausible claim orthographic form can affect literacy transfer it also establishes another plausible claim if english biliteracy is a major educational goal orthographic compatibility may matter what it does not establish strongly enough is therefore alphabetic signed writing is preferable to signwriting or other plane based systems that stronger recommendation depends on a chain of assumptions that the article does not sufficiently secure that signed writing should be optimized primarily for transfer into english that alphabetic signed writing would in fact provide the best such transfer that iconographic or spatial systems would create transfer burdens large enough to outweigh their writing internal adequacy that the educational and linguistic benefits of congruence with english outweigh other representational considerations those are not trivial assumptions the article moves across them too quickly it treats english biliteracy as the governing criterion one can see the deepest pressure point here grushkin is not only arguing for signed writing he is arguing for a signed writing system shaped by the needs of english biliteracy in alphabetic societies that makes english the silent norm around which the signed script should be designed this may be understandable in the context of deaf education it is still a limitation a signed writing system should first be judged by how well it serves signed language as writing only after that should additional questions be asked about transfer into majority language literacy grushkin s argument tends to reverse that order as a result the article is strongest where it says signed languages need writing and weakest where it says therefore alphabetic signed writing is the right answer the article does not adequately account for the written sign as a structured unit this is another important omission grushkin discusses writing system type literacy strategy and orthographic transfer but he does not sufficiently analyze the written sign as a unit of structure in systems such as signwriting the comparison is not really between pictures and letters it is between different models of what the primary written unit is and how written form is organized without a stronger structural account of the written sign the article s comparative framework remains too coarse it can say that one system looks more global and another more alphabetic but it cannot adequately explain what the spatial system is actually doing as writing that leaves the comparison underdescribed at exactly the point where it needs to be strongest the article s mixed treatment of education this is one of the article s more useful tensions grushkin is very good at identifying educational problems english centered pedagogy overreliance on gloss the difficulty of forcing deaf students to build literacy only through the majority written language the value of capitalizing on language strengths rather than deficits these are important insights but once the article reaches orthographic prescription the educational argument becomes too one directional the possibility that a well designed signed writing system might support literacy precisely because it is structurally adequate to signed language is never given equal weight with the goal of transfer into english the same asymmetry also makes it harder for the article to acknowledge that different systems may serve different educational roles a bridge script optimized for transfer a community handwriting practice and a full text infrastructure do not have to be the same thing that asymmetry matters it means the article treats signed writing partly as an educational bridge technology rather than fully as a native writing system in its own right again that does not ruin the whole article it does limit the force of the recommendation what the article actually establishes the article establishes several important things and these should be stated clearly its strongest responsible conclusions are signed languages benefit from writing common objections to written signed language are weak or overstated gloss is not an adequate long term written solution video does not replace writing as routine text technology signed writing could support literacy pedagogy communication preservation and internal discourse these are substantial contributions the article does not establish the stronger claims that that alphabetic signed writing should be preferred to signwriting that the iconographic label is enough to evaluate the structural adequacy of signed writing systems that compatibility with english literacy should govern the design of signed writing that visually organized signed writing systems are best understood through analogy with logography alone that difference matters the article s pro writing argument is strong its orthographic conclusion outruns the evidence offered review through the critique framework layer identification the article moves among writing system advocacy literacy and biliteracy theory comparative orthographic evaluation educational prescription those are not identical layers and the article does not always keep them distinct it tends to move from educational goals to writing system judgment too quickly declared dependencies help separate the two claims the necessity of writing argument solves a real advocacy and conceptual layer it shows why signed languages need durable writing rather than gloss or video alone the alphabetic orthographic recommendation however assumes layers that are not supplied a primary written unit adequate to signed language a text model conventions infrastructure and evidence that alphabetic compatibility should govern the design those layers are not merely deferred they are needed for the recommendation to carry the weight the article gives it representation model this is the central weakness the article evaluates signed writing under a spoken language orthographic expectation especially an alphabetic one that biases the comparison before the systems have been described at the appropriate structural level structural unit analysis weak the article does not adequately theorize the written sign as a structured unit in plane based systems as a result its treatment of signwriting remains too shallow to support the strength of its critique spatial vs linear organization mixed the article recognizes that signed languages are visual and spatial but in comparative judgment it tends to treat more linear horizontally arranged alphabetic organization as a practical advantage it does not seriously explore the possibility that spatially organized writing may be more adequate to the language even if less congruent with alphabetic habit text model and infrastructure limited the article acknowledges several actual signed writing systems but gives too little weight to signwriting as a mature structured already used system with real writing practice and infrastructure that weakens the comparative argument claims vs evidence this is the decisive issue the necessity of writing argument is stronger than the alphabetic recommendation the article s strongest pages justify writing its less supported pages prescribe a type of writing more confidently than the evidence allows main critique categories alphabetic bias incomplete infrastructure engagement full path overextension final assessment grushkin s article is important because it presses a real question that much of the literature avoids not whether signed languages can be written in principle but why signed languages should be written and what that writing is for its best contribution is clear signed languages need writing and the standard objections to written signed language are weak that argument remains valuable and deserves to be preserved its main weakness is equally clear it moves from the need for signed writing to an alphabetic prescription under an evaluative standard that is already biased toward alphabetic compatibility that is why the article is useful but not decisive it identifies the right problem it does not solve the second half of it well enough so the right verdict is neither dismissal nor acceptance without correction the article should be read as a strong argument for the necessity of signed writing a weaker argument for alphabetic signed writing and a clear example of how genuine insight can still be bent by alphabetic norms at the point of final recommendation

records.searchText: a review of martin 2000 review review for a review of martin 2000 martin is correct and useful as an early signwriting positive comparison but evidence limited as a full path claim from notation to writing and the threshold between descriptive systems and written text series signed language writing critical review martin 2000 from notation to writing primary signed language writing critical review series a review of martin 2000 review literature review essay working review martin 2000 from notation to writing a review of martin 2000 from notation to writing and the threshold between descriptive systems and written text reviewed source joe martin a linguistic comparison two notation systems for signed languages stokoe notation sutton signwriting year 2000 source type comparative paper joe martin s 2000 comparison of stokoe notation and sutton signwriting is worth keeping in view because it is sympathetic to signwriting in places where much of the literature was not that sympathy still matters the paper sees real things clearly signed writing is a real writing problem line based assumptions can distort the analysis signwriting is trying to be more than notation it also overstates what follows from those insights often enough to require correction executive summary martin compares stokoe notation and sutton signwriting across origins structure iconicity ordering literacy learnability grammar the problem of representing signed language in writing the paper s main strength is that it sees clearly that signed writing cannot be judged adequately by inherited spoken language norms alone it also takes signwriting seriously as a real public writing possibility rather than only as a research curiosity its main weakness is that it repeatedly turns real insight into stronger claims than the evidence shown can support framework judgment field judgment layer level judgment correct and useful as an early signwriting positive comparison beyond linear assumptions full path judgment useful but evidence limited as a full path claim because several historical literacy and learnability conclusions outrun the evidence shown main layer risk evidence overextension from real structural insight into broader claims about learning history and comparative adequacy what martin sees well it recognizes that signed writing is a real writing problem the paper does not treat signed language writing as impossible trivial or peripheral it treats the question seriously what kind of writing system is adequate to a signed language that remains a real strength it sees that linear assumptions create distortions one of the paper s strongest insights is that writing theories inherited from spoken language traditions can misdescribe signed language writing its discussion of spatial organization ordering simultaneous structure the limits of line based assumptions is often genuinely useful this is especially true where the paper contrasts signwriting s spatial organization with the more linear and taxonomic logic of stokoe style notation it takes signwriting seriously as more than notation the paper is favorable to signwriting in a way that much of the literature was not it does not dismiss signwriting as merely pictorial merely pedagogical or merely impractical it recognizes that signwriting is attempting something larger not just transcription but a usable writing system that is historically important and still worth preserving it identifies real limitations in stokoe style approaches the critique of stokoe style notation is not simply polemical the paper sees real problems in strict linear ordering limited treatment of non manuals alphabetic or taxonomic assumptions the difficulty of treating signed writing as though it were only a segmented sequence those are not imaginary problems at the same time the comparison is strongest when it keeps stokoe style systems in their proper niche stokoe notation was historically important because it made linguistic description of signed language more legible to researchers its limits as a public writing path do not erase its role as a research notation where the argument overextends the paper becomes less reliable when it turns these insights into broader conclusions than the evidence shown can support it overstates the limits of traditional linguistics the paper sometimes frames the issue as though traditional linguistic approaches are trapped by assumptions they cannot escape that claim is too strong the better conclusion is narrower some inherited spoken language assumptions distort signed writing analysis that is different from saying linguistics as such cannot handle the problem the stronger anti linguistics tone is historically understandable but analytically too broad it overstates what signwriting has already proven the paper repeatedly moves from signwriting is promising signwriting is feasible signwriting solves real structural problems to stronger claims such as signwriting is uniquely capable competing approaches face insurmountable obstacles signwriting is effectively the only script that can do the job those stronger claims need more discipline the paper s positive case for signwriting is real its strongest triumphal conclusions are not equally well established its learnability claims outrun the evidence shown the paper s discussion of learnability is one of the clearest places where the evidence standard weakens claims that deaf children can read signwriting without instruction or that signwriting alone can make that possible are not established strongly enough in the text as presented that does not mean the underlying intuition lacks value it means the empirical standard needs to be higher than anecdotal promise its software and typing sections are historically useful but dated the software discussion is a valuable historical snapshot it is not a stable present tense argument claims about typing software practicality and script usability need contemporary updating before they can be used as current evidence the software claims are therefore assessed against present day capability not treated as failures of the original 2000 paper this is not really a fault of the paper itself it is a reminder that a 2000 comparison cannot simply be imported unchanged into current technical discussions it does not always land cleanly on the written sign as the primary unit the paper is much better than many alternatives on writing space and structure but it still spends substantial time in the language of features parameters taxonomies comparative decomposition the review question here is not whether those discussions are useless they are not the question is whether the paper fully arrives at the stronger present day conclusion the written sign is the primary unit of signed text it moves in that direction but not always with full clarity what the paper actually establishes the strongest claims the paper supports are signwriting deserves to be treated as more than a research notation linear and alphabetic assumptions can distort signed writing analysis stokoe style notation has real limits as a public writing path spatial organization is not incidental to signed writing these are important claims what the paper does not fully prove is that traditional linguistic frameworks are simply unable to handle signed writing that signwriting has already resolved every literacy or usability question that competing approaches are decisively defeated in every case that anecdotal learnability observations are enough to settle the matter this distinction matters because the paper is strongest when it is clarifying structure it is weakest when it tries to convert structural insight directly into sweeping final verdicts review through the critique framework layer identification the paper is actually stronger than many review targets here it distinguishes notation writing system script orthography technical use popular use that is more sophisticated than many later accounts its weakness is not total layer blindness its weakness is that it still sometimes slides from one layer to another too quickly when making broader claims representation model this is one of the paper s strongest areas it sees clearly that signed writing cannot be evaluated only through alphabetic or speech derived expectations its treatment of spatial writing is often genuinely perceptive structural unit analysis this is more mixed the paper is better than symbol only or alphabetic approaches but it still does not always make the written sign itself the clearest primary unit of the discussion that is one of the main reasons the paper remains useful but not final spatial vs linear organization this is probably the paper s strongest analytical section it repeatedly sees what many other accounts miss that space is not a secondary display issue and that linearization can distort the writing problem itself text model and encoding historically interesting but now dated the software and typing discussion should be read as a snapshot of the moment not as a present tense technical baseline claims vs evidence this is where the review must be most careful the paper s insights are often real its conclusions are sometimes stronger than the evidence shown that is the main reason the full path judgment remains evidence limited even though several layer level contributions are correct and useful main critique categories evidence overextension level confusion forced linearization final assessment martin s paper is not important because it got everything right it is important because it saw several central problems clearly and argued for signwriting at a time when doing so still required breaking out of stronger received assumptions that deserves to be preserved at the same time the paper should not be treated as a final account its strongest continuing value is as an early signwriting positive comparison as a historically revealing argument for writing beyond linear assumptions as a sympathetic paper whose strongest insights still need more disciplined present day framing that is what makes it a strong review entry it shows that the series can do more than correct hostile or confused work it can also refine and strengthen work that was already moving in the right direction

records.title: A Review of Meletis and Dürscheid (2022) Review
records.summary: Review for A Review of Meletis and Dürscheid (2022). Meletis and Dürscheid are strong at general grapholinguistics but typologically incomplete for signed-language writing. Writing Systems and Their Use and the typological limit of signed language writing
records.parentTitle: A Review of Meletis and Dürscheid (2022)
keywords: Signed Language Writing Critical Review Series
A Review of Meletis and Dürscheid (2022)
records.contentExcerpt: A Review of Meletis and Dürscheid (2022) Writing Systems and Their Use and the typological limit of signed language writing Reviewed source: Dimitrios Meletis and Christa Dürscheid, Writing Systems and Their Use: An Overview of Grapholinguistics Publisher: De Gruyter Year: 2022 D...
records.searchText: a review of meletis and d rscheid 2022 review review for a review of meletis and d rscheid 2022 meletis and d rscheid are strong at general grapholinguistics but typologically incomplete for signed language writing writing systems and their use and the typological limit of signed language writing series signed language writing critical review meletis and durscheid 2022 writing systems and their use primary signed language writing critical review series a review of meletis and d rscheid 2022 review literature review essay working review meletis and durscheid 2022 writing systems and their use a review of meletis and d rscheid 2022 writing systems and their use and the typological limit of signed language writing reviewed source dimitrios meletis and christa d rscheid writing systems and their use an overview of grapholinguistics publisher de gruyter year 2022 doi 10 1515 9783110757835 dimitrios meletis and christa d rscheid s writing systems and their use is one of the stronger recent comparative accounts of writing a strong book is a better test of a field than a weak one if signed writing still strains the categories here the pressure probably is not local to one source the question in this review is straightforward once signed writing and signwriting are treated as real writing on their own terms is this framework broad enough a companion paper under this same entry asks the larger theory facing version of that question executive judgment framework judgment field judgment layer level judgment correct and useful as a strong general grapholinguistic framework full path judgment strong but typologically incomplete once signed writing is treated as a full comparative case with plane based written units authored spatial composition and signed text infrastructure main layer risk a general theory of writing remains too dependent on familiar spoken language writing categories when signed writing is treated as a pressure test refinement structurally strong but typologically incomplete this book does several things unusually well it rejects crude speech dependence gives writing internal structure takes visual form and materiality seriously and handles typology with more self awareness than much of the older literature those are substantial strengths and they are exactly what make the book worth pressing the deepest limitation is not that the book ignores writing structure it is that the framework still appears not to provide a stable typological place for writing systems whose primary written structure is articulatory spatial plane based and kinetic signwriting is exactly the kind of case that reveals whether the field s categories are merely broad enough for familiar systems or broad enough for writing itself what the book is trying to do writing systems and their use presents itself as a broad grapholinguistic account of writing rather than as a narrow descriptive handbook it aims to give writing serious comparative treatment across language speech and writing graphetics and materiality graphematics and graphematic units orthography writing system typology universals and wider comparative questions that ambition matters this is an attempt to define what counts as a serious modern theory of writing where the book is strongest it moves beyond the weakest speech dependence models one of the book s clearest strengths is that it does not simply repeat the old claim that writing is nothing but visible speech its discussion of language speech and writing as modalities is more careful than much of the older literature it distinguishes language from speech and writing treats the autonomy debate seriously and grants writing structures and functions that are not reducible to speech that is a real advance the review should not reduce the book to the weakest caricatures of writing theory it gives writing internal structure the treatment of graphematics is another genuine strength the book does not leave writing as a merely visual shell placed over linguistic substance it treats writing as a structured subsystem with its own units and relations graphematics graphotactics allography larger graphematic units and typology all receive real analytical weight it lets the book say more than writing corresponds to language it can also say writing has internal organization that deserves description in its own right it takes materiality and spatial organization seriously the sections on descriptive graphetics and materialisation are especially useful many theories of writing become thin precisely where writing becomes visual spatial material and socially embodied this book does better there it treats as analytically meaningful graphs and basic shapes spatial organization of the writing surface handwriting and typography readability legibility and processing the social meaning of visual variation that does not solve the signwriting problem by itself it does mean the book is not blind to the visual and spatial dimensions of writing it handles typology with real self awareness the typology chapter is stronger than many comparable accounts because it openly acknowledges its own difficulties the authors note that writing system typologies are often too broad based on only one criterion insufficiently fine grained prone to conflating primary and secondary representational levels their distinction between primary and secondary phonography and morphography is a real conceptual contribution it gives the field a more careful vocabulary than one finds in simpler typologies this matters because the book is not naively overconfident about its own classificatory tools where the book is most useful for signwriting discussion even where the framework does not yet go far enough it provides concepts that are genuinely helpful the autonomy discussion opens better ground than older models the treatment of dependence versus autonomy does not simply dissolve writing into speech that matters for signed language writing because it reopens the question of what writing can be when the language being written is not spoken the book does not fully answer that question but it does make it possible to ask it more cleanly graphetics and materiality make room for plane based writing the sections on spatial organization material embodiment handwriting and typography help prevent the theory of writing from becoming purely correspondence based they keep visible features of writing that often disappear in more phonology centered accounts that is one of the strongest points of contact for thinking about signwriting as authored visual text rather than as a translational aid or notation overlay the typology is good enough to make its own limit visible weak typologies fail so crudely that they reveal little beyond their own narrowness this book is better than that its typology is careful enough that the remaining problem becomes sharper it can describe many distinctions inside familiar writing traditions but it still may not have a primary category for a writing system whose authored structure is spatial and kinetic rather than primarily phonographic or morphographic where the framework begins to strain the book is strongest where it expands the field it is weakest where that expansion still does not seem expansive enough the typology still privileges familiar linguistic units for all its sophistication the main typological field of the book is still organized around relations between written units and linguistic units already familiar from speech shaped traditions phonemes syllables and moras morphemes that yields the major contrast between phonography and morphography alongside internal differentiation among segmentaries syllabaries abjads abugidas and related types this is a better and more self aware map than many earlier ones it may still not be broad enough the missing possibility is a writing type whose primary structural work is neither the durable encoding of sound nor the primary writing of morphemes but rather the durable encoding of articulatory spatial form that possibility does not seem to have a stable place in the present framework spatiality is acknowledged but still not typologically primary the book clearly knows that writing is spatial but spatiality still seems to function mostly as organization arrangement surface structure material presentation rather than as a primary representational type this distinction is decisive for signwriting spatial composition is not merely how the writing is laid out after the fact the plane is part of what is authored the written sign is not simply a linearly recoverable sequence that later receives spatial presentation the spatial arrangement is part of the written form itself if that is true then a theory that can speak about spatiality only as a surrounding property will eventually misdescribe the writing system it is trying to include signwriting is mentioned but the field is not reorganized around the challenge it poses the presence of signwriting in the book matters it shows that signed language writing is visible within the field but visibility is not the same as conceptual consequence signwriting does not appear to force the framework to revise its deepest typological assumptions instead signwriting appears more as something to be placed somewhere within the existing map than as something that might reveal the map itself to be incomplete chapter 2 modality and autonomy the opening chapters are among the most helpful parts of the book the authors distinguish language from the modalities in which it is realized and reject the simplest claim that writing is merely visible speech their position is better described as partial autonomy writing depends on language but it also has structures and functions not reducible to speech that is a serious improvement over older accounts it still leaves an unresolved question for signed language writing the unresolved question is whether writing is being theorized as dependent on language in a sufficiently modality neutral way or whether the structure of the discussion still quietly assumes speech shaped linguistic units as the default center of writing the book moves away from overt speech reductionism but the later typology and grapheme discussions suggest that the field s deeper defaults may remain harder to escape so chapter 2 should be preserved as a strength it should also be read as the place where the review s central pressure begins chapter 4 grapheme and graphematic units chapter 4 is probably the strongest single location in the book for comparative analytical seriousness the grapheme discussion is broad careful and explicitly cross linguistic the authors do not simply assume that graphemes must correspond to phonemes in an alphabetic sense they work through competing grapheme conceptions and build criteria meant to travel across different writing systems that is admirable work the pressure arises because even a broadened grapheme concept can still remain oriented toward relatively small units whose status is secured through correspondence to other linguistic units for many writing systems that is exactly the right place to begin for signwriting the central writing problem may not be captured fully at that level the question is not only what is the smallest distinctive written unit it is also what is the primary written unit that makes the text model possible for signwriting the answer may be the written sign rather than the grapheme like subunit if so the book s graphematic model remains powerful but not sufficient larger graphematic units and the problem of the written sign the discussion of larger graphematic units adds another pressure point the book s treatment of graphematic syllables and graphematic words is subtle and often useful particularly in the way it distinguishes writing internal structure from mere reflection of phonological units it also handles cross script differences more carefully than many simpler accounts still the overall unit hierarchy remains shaped by the kinds of structures that are already legible within established writing system theory for signwriting the likely problem is not that the hierarchy has no larger units it is that the relevant larger unit may not be well described by imported expectations about wordhood syllabic layering or line based segmentation if the written sign is the primary durable unit of signed text then the theory may need not merely a more flexible grapheme but a different center of gravity reading processes useful evidence limited transfer the psycholinguistic discussion is stronger than many comparable theoretical books because it explicitly asks whether grapholinguistic concepts have psychological correlates that is the right question it matters that the book tries to connect graph basic shape grapheme allography graphotactics to actual processing at the same time the reading literature it mobilizes remains heavily shaped by word recognition alphabetic assumptions phonology heavy traditions english centered model building the book explicitly acknowledges this alphabetism which is to its credit but acknowledgment does not fully solve the transfer problem a framework whose processing discussion remains deeply tied to alphabetic word recognition may have difficulty accommodating signed language literacy especially if signed writing depends on different perceptual and unit level dynamics than the literature it cites was built to describe kinematography as a pressure test this is where a candidate term such as kinematography becomes useful i do not mean its historical overlap with cinematography and the term is not presented here as settled field vocabulary i mean it as a candidate typological term for writing systems whose primary structural work is the durable encoding of articulatory spatial kinetic form placed beside phonography morphography it names a third possibility used carefully kinematography would denote a writing type in which written units primarily preserve the kinematic structure of signing handshape location movement orientation spatial relations this is not offered here as a settled final label or as a complete typological proposal it is offered as a pressure test if a term like this becomes necessary in order to describe what signwriting is doing structurally then the current typology is not merely awkward at one edge it is missing a class of writing system organization that would be a major conclusion it would mean the limitation is not just classificatory inconvenience it is typological incompleteness what the book actually establishes the strongest claims the book supports are writing is not reducible to speech writing has internal structure deserving independent analysis visual and material form matter to the study of writing typology must distinguish primary and secondary representational levels comparative grapholinguistics is possible and worth pursuing those claims should be preserved what the book does not yet appear to establish is that its present typology is sufficient for all writing systems that signed language writing can be housed cleanly inside the phonography morphography field as currently drawn that spatiality has already been granted the correct status when it becomes part of the primary authored form final assessment this book should not be reviewed as though it were just one more simplistic account that writing equals speech written down that would be analytically weak it should be reviewed as one of the strongest current attempts to theorize writing comparatively and precisely for that reason as a framework worth pressing at its limits its deepest contribution is that it gives writing real internal structure its deepest limitation is that it still appears not to grant signed language writing a fully adequate typological position that is why the best judgment is not simple rejection more specifically a strong grapholinguistic framework that remains typologically incomplete once signed language writing is treated as a real writing type rather than as a marginal special case that is what makes the book both valuable and reviewable
