records.title: Iconicity, Convention, and Legibility Paper
records.summary: Paper for Iconicity, Convention, and Legibility. Visual motivation, convention, and legibility show how iconic resources become disciplined writing in SignWriting. How motivated visual form becomes disciplined writing
keywords: Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting
records.contentExcerpt: Iconicity, Convention, and Legibility How motivated visual form becomes disciplined writing This paper develops the legibility and convention argument in Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting. It follows Perspective and Authored Space and prepares the ground for Handwr...
records.searchText: iconicity convention and legibility paper paper for iconicity convention and legibility visual motivation convention and legibility show how iconic resources become disciplined writing in signwriting how motivated visual form becomes disciplined writing series writing and representation iconicity convention and legibility primary writing and representation in sutton signwriting iconicity convention and legibility paper theoretical paper working paper iconicity convention and legibility iconicity convention and legibility how motivated visual form becomes disciplined writing this paper develops the legibility and convention argument in writing and representation in sutton signwriting it follows perspective and authored space and prepares the ground for handwriting and spatial script practice the earlier papers make three claims writing does not have to be linear signwriting is best described with both a representational axis and an organizational axis the writer authors viewpoint relation and composed space inside the written sign this paper asks the next question if signwriting is visually motivated why is it writing rather than drawing the answer is not that signwriting has no iconicity the answer is the opposite iconicity is not the opposite of writing iconicity becomes compatible with writing when motivated visual form is stabilized by convention contrast repetition teachability correction and reader practice that is the central claim of this paper the common mistake signwriting is easy to misread at first glance it contains visible traces of bodies hands movement faces contact and space a hand symbol may resemble a hand a movement arrow may suggest a path a facial symbol may point toward an expression or body location the signbox may look like a small visual scene because of that some readers make a quick jump if it looks like what it represents it must be drawing rather than writing that conclusion is too fast the question is not whether a written form has visual motivation the question is what kind of system controls that motivation free drawing can invent resemblance locally writing stabilizes form through shared conventions the issue is not iconic or arbitrary the better issue is locally improvised or conventionally disciplined signwriting belongs on the second side of that distinction iconicity is not a disqualification many writing systems and notational systems contain traces of motivation some letterforms have historical visual origins some scripts preserve graphic analogy mathematical notation uses spatial and visual relations that are not purely arbitrary musical notation uses vertical placement repeated symbols stems beams rests and other visible conventions that are learned as part of a system maps and diagrams use motivated visual relations although they are not all language writing the presence of motivation does not settle the classification motivated form can appear in illustration notation diagramming writing interface design symbolic art the classification depends on how the form works for writing the crucial questions are are forms repeatable are distinctions shared are contrasts teachable can readers learn what counts as the same or different can a writer be corrected can texts be copied stored compared searched and reused does the system support durable language bearing text those questions move the discussion away from a shallow opposition between iconicity and writing they ask how iconicity is conventionalized they also ask what the iconicity is doing that question matters because a visible form can be motivated without being language writing iconicity is not the same as pictography writing systems theory often distinguishes language writing from broader visible meaning making street signs pictograms emoji interface icons maps and diagrams can communicate through visual resemblance or convention they can be useful stable and widely understood but they are not automatically writing in the narrower language writing sense the difference is not that they lack convention many of them are highly conventional the difference is that they often point directly to ideas actions affect places or situations rather than writing a language form as text that distinction matters for signwriting signwriting is not primarily a set of icons for ideas it writes signed language forms its symbols are visually motivated by hands movement facial expression body location and spatial relation but those motivated symbols are used to compose written signs that belong to signed language this keeps the argument from sliding into a weaker claim signwriting is writing because pictures can communicate that is not the claim the stronger claim is signwriting is writing because visually motivated symbols are conventionalized into durable language bearing written forms iconicity can help explain why some symbols look the way they do it does not by itself explain why the system is writing for that the system must support written identity contrast composition correction reading and reuse what convention does convention is not decoration placed on top of writing convention is what lets writing become shareable in signwriting convention turns visually motivated marks into a usable writing system by making forms repeatable teachable contrastive correctable comparable searchable legible across writers durable across media this is why a symbol that resembles a hand is not merely a hand picture inside the system it is a selected written symbol it has identity it has contrast with other symbols it participates in fill rotation placement movement and relation conventions it can be copied it can be encoded it can be searched it can be corrected when the wrong symbol or relation is used correctable does not mean governed by a single global authority it means that within a community teaching tradition dictionary classroom corpus or publication context readers and writers can recognize when a written form works when it does not and when another form would be clearer that is what convention does it does not remove visual motivation it disciplines it more precisely convention gives visual motivation a role inside a system it answers questions a drawing can leave open question why it matters what counts as the same symbol without symbol identity forms drift into private drawing what counts as a contrast without contrast readers cannot know which differences matter what counts as a valid placement without placement conventions spatial relation becomes guesswork what counts as a complete written sign without boundaries texts become loose clusters of marks what can be corrected without correctability instruction and editing remain unstable what can be preserved without preservation text cannot reliably move across media these questions are not merely technical they are writing systems questions they describe the path from visual motivation to written form how convention emerges convention in signwriting is not a central authority deciding the one correct written form for every sign the same symbol set and encoding model can support many sign languages and many writing traditions writers choose how to write a sign readers decide whether that writing is readable useful and worth accepting communities schools dictionaries corpora and repeated use then shape which written forms become common this is bottom up convention a text entry system may show existing written signs but those signs are examples available to the writer not rules imposed on the writer the writer may reuse an existing form adapt it or write a new form over time useful forms recur unclear awkward or idiosyncratic forms tend to remain rare in this sense signwriting behaves like a living writing system good writing is not defined globally by decree it emerges through use the signwriting case the signed writing system considered here is sutton signwriting in signwriting iconicity and convention meet at multiple layers layer iconicity role convention role hand symbols motivated by visible hand form the symbol inventory stabilizes distinctions among handshapes fill and rotation motivated by palm facing and orientation fill and rotation categories make orientation readable and comparable movement arrows motivated by path direction and movement type arrow conventions define how motion is represented in writing facial and body symbols motivated by visible expression and body location symbol classes and placement stabilize what is selected for writing signbox placement motivated by signed space and body relation spatial conventions turn placement into written relation sign boundaries motivated by the composed sign as a unit boundaries let signs become text units passage layout motivated by reading flow and visual organization columns lanes and orientation stabilize passage reading no single layer proves that signwriting is writing the system matters as a whole a hand symbol alone is not the writing system a movement arrow alone is not the writing system a signbox alone is not the writing system the writing system emerges from the disciplined relation among symbol inventory placement viewpoint movement boundaries passage layout and reader practice that is why signwriting should not be reduced to either of two oversimplifications an intuitive picture system an arbitrary technical code it is neither it is visually motivated writing governed by learned conventions legibility is learned contrast legibility is often misunderstood as immediate visual recognition that misunderstanding matters here a novice may see a cluster of marks and think this looks like hands and movement a reader sees something more specific this handshape not that one this palm facing not that one this rotation not that one this movement path not that one this location relation not that one this facial or body symbol not that one this sign boundary not a loose collection of symbols legibility is not raw resemblance it is trained recognition inside a system of contrast this is true far beyond signwriting readers of alphabetic scripts do not merely see lines and curves they see letters variants ligatures punctuation word boundaries and acceptable or unacceptable forms readers of musical notation do not merely see dots and stems they see pitch duration grouping meter phrasing and performance instructions readers of mathematical notation do not merely see marks scattered on a page they see exponentiation grouping fraction structure matrices variables operators and scope the same principle applies to signwriting the writing is visible but its legibility is learned iconicity may help a new reader approach the system it does not replace literacy this distinction can be stated sharply transparency is not the same as legibility a transparent form looks motivated to a viewer a legible form is recognized within a learned system of written distinctions some signwriting symbols may be relatively transparent to beginners others may not be even transparent symbols become fully legible only when the reader knows what differences matter that is why the system should not be evaluated by asking only what an untrained viewer can guess an untrained viewer may guess something useful that does not mean the viewer is reading reading begins when visual recognition is disciplined by learned contrast iconicity and abstraction it would be a mistake to say that every part of signwriting is transparent some forms are more visually obvious than others some are highly conventional some require instruction before they become legible some distinctions may be difficult for beginners but important for fluent reading and writing this is normal writing systems do not become less real because they contain abstraction they also do not become less real because some forms are motivated signwriting sits between two bad descriptions fully pictorial and immediately obvious fully arbitrary and unrelated to visible signing the better description is signwriting uses motivated visual resources within a conventional writing system that means iconicity is uneven it varies by symbol class use context reader experience teaching tradition and text type some hand symbols may be visually approachable some movement conventions may need explicit instruction some spatial placements may become clear only after a reader understands viewpoint and signbox conventions this unevenness is not a weakness in the typological argument it is part of the system s actual writing behavior it also prevents a second overclaim signwriting s iconicity should not be treated as a guarantee of effortless literacy motivation may support learning memory or initial orientation those are plausible possibilities but they require evidence and the learnability question belongs primarily in sutton signwriting language and literacy research rather than in this writing systems argument the writing systems claim is narrower and stronger whether or not iconicity makes learning easier in a measurable way it does not disqualify signwriting as writing that keeps the argument where this paper can support it from resemblance to written difference the strongest test is not whether a symbol resembles something the strongest test is whether the system can make written differences a writing system must support contrast in signwriting contrast can appear through choosing one handshape rather than another changing palm facing or rotation using a different movement arrow moving a symbol within the signbox adding or omitting facial body information changing the relation among symbols changing sign boundaries or passage layout those are written differences they may correspond to differences in signed form reading expectation lexical identity grammatical marking or textual presentation depending on context the important point for this paper is narrower visual motivation becomes writing when differences are conventional enough to be selected read corrected and reused this is why the phrase just drawing misses the point a drawing can be expressive without having a stable system of written contrast signwriting depends on contrast this contrastive test is also where signwriting differs from many pictorial or emoji like uses a reader may interpret an emoji differently depending on context tone platform rendering or local habit that flexibility can be communicatively powerful but it is not the same as a writing system in which recurring graphic distinctions are tied to a structured written form of language signwriting allows interpretation too but it also asks whether the writer selected the right symbol orientation placement movement relation and boundary those are not merely expressive options they are part of the written text what convention does not solve by itself convention is necessary it is not magic a conventional system can still have open questions which distinctions matter most for fluent reading which symbol choices vary across writers or teaching traditions how much spatial variation can readers tolerate which conventions are stable across signed languages and which are local which forms are easy for beginners and which require sustained instruction how do handwritten forms differ from font rendered forms these questions should be studied rather than hidden they belong partly in sutton signwriting language and literacy research partly in handwriting and spatial script practice and partly in evidence building around actual readers and texts the presence of open questions does not reduce signwriting to illustration it means signwriting should be studied as a real writing system real writing systems have variation learning curves local norms and histories of stabilization relation to plane based writing the first native paper in this series plane based writing and script typology defined plane based writing as writing in which the primary written unit is composed through spatial relation within a two dimensional field this paper adds an important condition plane based writing requires legible convention a plane by itself is not writing a spatial arrangement by itself is not writing an iconic mark by itself is not writing writing begins when selected forms and relations are stabilized enough for a reader to recover a durable written object for signwriting that means the signbox is not only a spatial field it is a conventional writing field the writer does not merely place marks wherever they seem visually pleasant the writer works inside a system of symbol choices viewpoint conventions relation conventions and layout practices this is why iconicity and plane based composition belong together the plane gives the system a spatial organization convention makes that organization readable relation to authored space the preceding paper perspective and authored space argued that the writer authors viewpoint placement relation and composed space this paper explains why that authorship can be legible authored space does not mean private space it does not mean the writer invents a visual solution from nothing each time authorship happens inside convention the writer chooses but the choices are meaningful because readers share the system that is true in all writing alphabetic writers choose words punctuation capitalization spacing and sometimes layout those choices are authored but they are intelligible because they take place inside shared conventions signwriting writers choose symbols viewpoint placement relation and layout those choices are authored too and they become legible because the system gives readers ways to interpret them authorship and convention are not opposites convention is what makes authorship readable relation to machine encoding the following technical bridge paper human writing and machine encoding asks how authored signed writing can be stored and processed by machines this paper prepares that question by clarifying what must be preserved encoding does not need to preserve a vague visual impression it needs to preserve conventional written distinctions that includes symbol identity orientation relevant distinctions signbox placement sign boundaries movement and relation symbols enough structure for rendering search comparison and reuse if iconic writing were merely drawing encoding would only need to store an image but if iconic writing is conventional writing encoding must preserve the structured text behind the visible form that is why the distinction matters technically as well as theoretically what this paper claims this paper makes five claims first iconicity is not the opposite of writing second visual motivation becomes writing when it is stabilized by shared conventions third signwriting is not free illustration because its symbols and spatial relations participate in a learned system of contrast fourth legibility depends on trained recognition not raw resemblance fifth convention makes authored spatial writing preservable teachable correctable and technically supportable scope and next questions the claim here is about how visual motivation becomes readable writing through convention that scope leaves room for several necessary follow up questions novice transparency reading fluency community specific conventions handwriting practice educational outcomes and comparisons with pictograms maps diagrams interface icons and other spatial symbol systems all require their own evidence those questions remain important and they belong in the writing systems evidence and research series where they can be handled directly conclusion signwriting should not be defended by denying its iconicity its visual motivation is part of its representational strength the stronger argument is that iconicity alone is not the deciding category for writing convention is what makes visual motivation legible as part of a shared system when motivated forms become stable contrastive teachable correctable and reusable they can become writing that is what signwriting demonstrates it shows that a writing system can preserve visible features of signed language without collapsing into free drawing it also shows that a plane based writing system requires more than spatial placement it requires legible convention from here the next question is technical but not merely technical how can those conventional written distinctions be preserved as machine readable text that is the bridge first to handwriting and spatial script practice and then to human writing and machine encoding selected references coulmas f 1989 the writing systems of the world blackwell daniels p t bright w eds 1996 the world s writing systems oxford university press goodman n 1976 languages of art an approach to a theory of symbols 2nd ed hackett meletis d d rscheid c 2022 writing systems and their use an overview of grapholinguistics de gruyter sampson g 1985 writing systems a linguistic introduction stanford university press slevinski s 2026 human writing and machine encoding authorship serialization and the layers of signed text in writing and representation in sutton signwriting slevinski s 2026 perspective and authored space viewpoint placement and the authored space of signed writing in writing and representation in sutton signwriting slevinski s 2026 plane based writing and script typology where signwriting fits and where existing writing system categories strain in writing and representation in sutton signwriting sutton v 1995 lessons in signwriting deaf action committee for signwriting sutton v 2010 iswa 2010 international signwriting alphabet center for sutton movement writing

records.title: Infrastructure and Stewardship White Paper
records.summary: White Paper for Infrastructure and Stewardship. Sutton SignWriting has real infrastructure assets, active stewardship, and continuity risks that justify a stronger accountable structure. Confirmed assets, current risks, and the case for stronger stewardship structure
records.contentExcerpt: Infrastructure and Stewardship Confirmed assets, current risks, and the case for stronger stewardship structure This brief states the asset base, continuity risks, and stewardship structure for Sutton SignWriting. The issue is no longer whether SignWriting infrastructure exists,...
records.searchText: infrastructure and stewardship white paper white paper for infrastructure and stewardship sutton signwriting has real infrastructure assets active stewardship and continuity risks that justify a stronger accountable structure confirmed assets current risks and the case for stronger stewardship structure series infrastructure and program infrastructure and stewardship primary sutton signwriting infrastructure and program infrastructure and stewardship white paper white paper white paper infrastructure and stewardship infrastructure and stewardship confirmed assets current risks and the case for stronger stewardship structure this brief states the asset base continuity risks and stewardship structure for sutton signwriting the issue is no longer whether signwriting infrastructure exists but whether it will remain stable under a stronger stewardship model sutton signwriting already operates as infrastructure the main assets the work currently required to keep them running and the main risks are visible enough that a pi administrator or collaborator can see what a host institution would actually be stewarding overview sutton signwriting is existing infrastructure not a speculative project the system already includes domains and public websites legacy and active servers software and code maintained across multiple languages dictionaries datasets and corpus assets formal encoding standards and rendering systems the challenge is no longer whether these things can be created the challenge is whether they will be kept running documented clearly and given stronger stewardship backing before the maintenance burden outgrows the current model in program terms the work is a combination of preservation maintenance modernization and research enablement asset category examples stewardship need public web infrastructure domains websites documentation sites renewal hosting migration monitoring server infrastructure legacy services production applications static hosting backups deployment documentation modernization software and code libraries fonts rendering tools web applications maintenance compatibility onboarding data and corpus assets signpuddle datasets dictionaries written materials preservation metadata access planning standards and encoding iswa 2010 fsw swu fonts specification maintenance implementation support partner infrastructure international use cases partner statements research contacts coordination documentation continuity domains and web infrastructure the signwriting ecosystem includes a coordinated set of domains that support public access tools documentation archival material and related stewardship work not every stewarded domain is part of the core sutton signwriting infrastructure the public asset register distinguishes core related and adjacent domains core signwriting access tool and documentation domains under active maintenance include signwriting org signwriting net signwriting com signpuddle org signpuddle net signpuddle com signbank org signbank net sutton signwriting io related sutton and movementwriting domains include valeriesutton org dancewriting org movementwriting org adjacent or historical stewarded domains include aslgospel org soulsite com pamelasuttonmd org paulmsutton org steveslevinski me these domains represent the primary public presence of signwriting application entry points such as signpuddle and signmaker related services technical documentation and standards resources historical and archival materials keeping these domains running includes domain renewal and continuity of ownership dns and hosting configuration uptime and monitoring content preservation and migration planning this is already a real web infrastructure portfolio server and platform infrastructure the ecosystem currently depends on a combination of legacy signwriting servers active production servers static and dynamic web hosting environments these systems support signpuddle and dictionary services signwriting org and archival content developer resources and documentation data access and downloads key characteristics include long running systems with historical continuity a mixed technology stack including php javascript python and static hosting evolving architecture with ongoing redevelopment in platform v3 immediate operational concerns include backup and disaster recovery migration planning for legacy systems documentation of deployment and configuration reduction of single person operational dependency this is exactly the kind of infrastructure that becomes more valuable as its maintenance risk becomes more visible concrete operational pressure the continuity risk is no longer only theoretical recent international dictionary and community use has exposed scaling maintenance and legacy system risks public facing characterization is enough here the incidents involved service interruptions high traffic pressure legacy maintenance and recovery work that required direct maintainer attention these incidents required direct maintainer intervention and demonstrate that the continuity risk is already practical rather than theoretical detailed incident records security sensitive information and mitigation plans belong in internal due diligence materials for host grant and migration planning rather than in the public release file these incidents matter because they show that thin stewardship is already interrupting real international work not merely raising abstract maintenance concerns software and code infrastructure the signwriting ecosystem includes a substantial body of maintained code across multiple languages primary development is organized through the sutton signwriting github organization and related repositories including core libraries in javascript python and php rendering and font systems web components and client applications api and platform development tools such as signmaker signpuddle viewers and related utilities key characteristics include cross language implementation open source licensing active development and maintenance production use in tools websites and research contexts this is a mature technical foundation not a prototype environment data and corpus assets one of the most significant infrastructure components is the accumulated signwriting data this includes signpuddle dictionaries and datasets spml signpuddle markup language files millions of sign entries across languages structured lexical and textual resources literature collections including educational and religious texts key characteristics include community generated and internationally sourced data accumulation over roughly two decades preservation through multiple format migrations partial normalization and structure already present this dataset represents a rare large scale corpus of written signed language immediate stewardship concerns include long term preservation format standardization metadata and indexing controlled access pathways for research use for an academic host that is not just a maintenance burden it is also a serious research asset standards and encoding infrastructure the technical foundation of signwriting includes formal encoding systems and specifications international signwriting alphabet 2010 iswa 2010 formal signwriting in ascii fsw signwriting in unicode swu associated fonts and rendering systems these systems enable machine readable signwriting support searching sorting and processing integrate with modern web and programming environments together they represent the supported v1 0 0 production framework for machine readable sutton signwriting stewardship responsibilities include maintaining compatibility across formats documenting specifications supporting implementation across tools and languages preserving the link between formal standards and production systems this standards layer is one of the clearest reasons to treat the work as long term scholarly and technical infrastructure continuity and responsibility current responsibility follows a recognizable lineage valerie sutton as originator of sutton signwriting and foundational system design steve slevinski as technical steward encoding architect and infrastructure maintainer a designated next generation technical path for future continuity this continuity includes technical knowledge transfer operational responsibilities across domains servers and code preservation of standards datasets and public facing systems the issue is not that continuity is absent the issue is that the current stewardship model is still too informal for infrastructure of this scope and importance risk profile with a clearer internal view the primary risks can be stated more directly concentration of operational knowledge dependence on a very small number of maintainers incomplete documentation of systems and workflows limited institutional redundancy long term preservation risk for data domains and services these are typical risks for infrastructure that has grown in value faster than the stewardship around it risk current impact first mitigation single maintainer dependency continuity risk for domains services data and technical decisions documentation plus a backup maintainer path legacy server continuity risk outages or slowdowns under real use bot pressure and cpu heavy requests monitoring rate limiting caching and migration planning data preservation loss corruption or inaccessible archives would weaken research and community use scheduled backups checksums and recovery testing domain continuity public resources could become unstable or difficult to recover renewal calendar ownership documentation continuity plan documentation gaps new collaborators cannot onboard reliably deployment architecture and workflow documentation partner confidence international collaborators may hesitate if infrastructure looks informal clear stewardship model status reporting and service priorities the situation is not unusual because the work is weak it is unusual because the work has become significant without yet receiving the stronger host structure it requires why this matters for a host institution this level of asset maturity changes the institutional question a host institution would not be funding an idea starting from zero being asked to invent relevance after the fact it would be stabilizing and extending an existing documented international infrastructure system inheriting a research ready and partnership ready technical base making preservation maintenance and future grants more credible supporting preservation maintenance modernization and research enablement as one connected program this reduces startup cost and increases grant readiness collaboration potential research applicability educational and public impact value relationship to platform v3 platform v3 means the next integrated web and application architecture for sutton signwriting tools services accounts and data workflows it is the next architectural layer not the starting point the existing infrastructure already includes domains systems code data and standards in practical terms platform v3 would modernize the service layer around account and access patterns shared data workflows deployment reliability public interfaces and research ready integration points it is the place where old public resources current libraries dictionary workflows and future ai or corpus facing tools can be made easier to maintain together platform v3 is the opportunity to modernize the service layer improve user registration and access patterns support institutional and educational use more effectively create a better base for ai translation and research workflows in other words platform v3 extends the infrastructure it does not create it from nothing for the shortest stewardship summary see executive brief for the research facing bridge into study design and cumulative science see sutton signwriting language and literacy research research program for literacy without sound

records.title: Infrastructure Core and Philanthropy Brief
records.summary: Brief for Infrastructure Core and Philanthropy. A focused SignWriting infrastructure core could stabilize existing tools, standards, data, translation, coordination, and international access work. A stewardship model for staffing, translation, coordination, and continuity
records.contentExcerpt: Infrastructure Core and Philanthropy A stewardship model for staffing, translation, coordination, and continuity Sutton SignWriting requires grant-ready research pathways. It also requires continuity capacity that grants alone often do not provide. That is where an infrastructure...
records.searchText: infrastructure core and philanthropy brief brief for infrastructure core and philanthropy a focused signwriting infrastructure core could stabilize existing tools standards data translation coordination and international access work a stewardship model for staffing translation coordination and continuity series infrastructure and program infrastructure core and philanthropy primary sutton signwriting infrastructure and program infrastructure core and philanthropy brief brief program brief infrastructure core and philanthropy infrastructure core and philanthropy a stewardship model for staffing translation coordination and continuity sutton signwriting requires grant ready research pathways it also requires continuity capacity that grants alone often do not provide that is where an infrastructure core and philanthropy become useful status note this entry describes possible stewardship and funding pathways it does not announce a donation program fiscal sponsor host institution gift policy or legal receiving structure the core idea is simple create a small focused stewardship structure around existing signwriting standards software data public resources translation needs and international coordination this is not a proposal to invent a new system it is a proposal to stabilize document and extend infrastructure that already exists why an infrastructure core is needed the current signwriting ecosystem includes real assets standards fonts software libraries public websites dictionaries and datasets written materials international user relationships the problem is that these assets remain too dependent on informal maintenance and limited continuity capacity an infrastructure core would make the stewardship function explicit it would give the work a place to hold technical stewardship documentation data preservation partner coordination translation and access research readiness continuity planning what the core would do a small infrastructure core could support function practical work standards maintenance keep iswa fsw swu fonts and related documentation coherent software stewardship maintain libraries tools services and platform v3 development data stewardship preserve dictionaries corpora metadata and migration pathways translation and access identify high value works secure permissions and support reviewed translations global coordination support partners local liaisons statements of use and follow up research integration help convert infrastructure into usable study materials corpora and grants succession planning reduce single maintainer dependency through documentation and shared capacity this is a high leverage stewardship layer it is intentionally smaller than a large center translation and access translation and access deserve special attention some important signwriting materials already exist in portuguese and other languages but remain difficult for english centric academic audiences to use a disciplined translation and access program could identify priority works secure permission from authors publishers or rights holders use ai assisted translation only as a draft support tool require human editorial review return finished translations in forms approved by the original authors or communities support online hosting print or co publication where appropriate this should not become extractive translation the goal is to make important work more accessible while respecting authors communities rights and local context the program should translate selectively beginning with the most important and most permission ready materials why philanthropy fits philanthropy fits because signwriting infrastructure sits between usual funding categories it is too technical for many humanities only funding routes too language and community centered for purely technical funding too long term for short grant cycles alone too operational for research grants to carry comfortably by themselves philanthropy can support the continuity layer that grants often underfund staffing documentation maintenance translation coordination bridge funding match support for grants that does not make philanthropy a replacement for grants it makes philanthropy a stabilizing partner gift and continuity scenarios exact budgets should be developed with a host institution or fiscal sponsor still the continuity tiers can be described generically tier what it supports result continuity capacity hosting backups documentation domain continuity reduces immediate collapse risk stabilization capacity part time technical help data preservation platform maintenance makes the infrastructure easier to sustain program capacity core staff time translation access work partner coordination turns stewardship into a visible program growth capacity grant matching student research assistant support platform v3 acceleration makes broader research and collaboration more realistic a large philanthropic gift could combine several tiers smaller gifts can still be useful if they are tied to concrete continuity or access tasks gift and donor safeguards no public donation solicitation should begin unless a legal receiving pathway exists through a host institution fiscal sponsor nonprofit or other accountable structure gift acceptance policy restricted versus unrestricted gift terms reporting obligations and conflict of interest handling belong with that receiving structure donors should not control research conclusions community review outcomes technical validity spelling or language authority or the governance of affected communities donors should also not receive privileged access to data corpora signer media or dataset exports as consideration for gifts relationship to grants the strongest funding model is layered grants are strongest for research studies corpus development computational methods literacy and reading research accessibility and education projects platform development tied to research aims philanthropy is strongest for bridge staffing translation and access continuity and maintenance planning time before grants unfunded infrastructure follow through partner coordination that does not fit neatly into one grant the two routes should reinforce each other philanthropy can make grants more credible by keeping the infrastructure stable enough for researchers to build on it grants can make philanthropy more credible by showing concrete research outcomes practical first promise the infrastructure core idea should make a practical first promise before it makes larger claims it is not a promise of universal adoption immediate literacy outcomes instant institutional maturity full translation of all important materials one permanent solution to all maintenance needs its first promise is concrete make the existing infrastructure more stable and more usable for research education preservation and international collaboration that is enough bottom line sutton signwriting requires research funding and it also requires the stewardship layer that makes research funding usable an infrastructure core gives that layer a name philanthropy gives it one realistic continuity path together they can turn thin continuity into a stronger base for standards software data translation access and international collaboration
