donations to move through a pi and institutional structure rather than through an improvised arrangement without that first structure every later conversation becomes more difficult people may still be interested in the work but the next step remains vague a pi or lab base turns a vague future into an actionable path institutional base first support can grow the first decision is an institutional base and affiliation funding can grow around that base through project support partial capacity support grants gifts or future infrastructure funding that model means the institutional base comes first sustained capacity can grow around the base the first institutional decision remains smaller than the full future vision this is a reduced friction model the immediate ask is disciplined the work already has value support can grow around the base the first institutional step remains smaller than the full future structure the larger structure can then grow around that base through several reinforcing continuity paths partial capacity support project based dms plan workflow and repository work grants under the pi gifts and donations as bridge support longer term research infrastructure development the strength of this model is that it does not require one conversation or one funding source to solve everything at once what the pi is actually being asked to do the pi is being asked to provide a pi lab or unit relationship academic affiliation and credibility a real university base for future work this is important because it keeps the ask clear the ask is provide the first accountable academic location so useful work can begin now and broader support can grow around demonstrated value why a pi benefits the strongest version of this proposal is a practical value proposition for the pi and lab immediate benefit steve can help with current and near term research needs especially data management and sharing plan development and revision workflow implementation across complex project systems repository preparation and submission support technical help in the space between local data systems and external delivery standards guidance where structured data and documentation are needed this means the lab gains useful operational capacity rather than only taking on an additional administrative relationship the fuller operational case belongs in research team value medium term benefit once steve is institutionally placed the pi gains a stronger base for cross project dms and repository support collaborative problem solving around data workflows and implementation grant concept development additional faculty and project conversations building a broader set of collaborations around steve s skills long term benefit the lab also becomes the place from which a broader research infrastructure effort can grow that longer arc includes sutton signwriting support software maintenance data and corpus infrastructure collaboration across literacy language accessibility and research systems the point is not that the pi must immediately own the whole future initiative the point is that the pi becomes the person who made the future possible by supporting the right first step what steve brings immediately the case for a pi or lab base is stronger because steve already brings practical value now his near term usefulness includes shaping dms plans into real workflows helping teams organize data from mixed systems supporting validation naming and repository preparation helping projects finish difficult delivery work under real deadlines this is especially relevant for projects that use combinations of redcap spreadsheets and csv files technical system outputs imaging related data external collaborators or vendors repositories such as nda the practical value here is not theoretical it is the kind of work that helps projects avoid confusion reduce delay and finish difficult repository and compliance tasks successfully the continuity model the long term strength of this plan comes from having several reinforcing continuity paths instead of one all or nothing path these paths matter because the first base does not have to carry the whole future by itself path 1 pi or lab base the first requirement is a pi or lab base this is the anchor for everything else path 2 partial capacity support advocates and collaborators may be able to help secure part of the ongoing capacity needed for steve s work this matters because it lowers the immediate burden on any one source creates runway gives the arrangement more stability while broader support develops path 3 dms plan workflow and repository work steve s practical usefulness can create relationships across the university through dms plan support workflow implementation repository delivery work technical collaboration around data organization and validation this creates visible value before the larger signwriting infrastructure vision needs to be fully developed path 4 grant writing under the pi once steve is institutionally based grants can be written under the pi to support steve s work project facing dms plan workflow and repository work software and documentation maintenance broader sutton signwriting infrastructure future research collaboration path 5 gifts and donations there is also a complementary bridge continuity path through gifts and donations it can support bridge support follow through support infrastructure support it works best as a disciplined complement to grants and sustained capacity not as a substitute for them why broader university relationships matter the pi or lab base is the anchor but the model works best when steve is also visible and useful beyond a single room or single project that matters for several reasons some researchers need immediate dms help now some teams need practical repository delivery support now some collaborators may become future co investigators or advocates some units may be able to contribute sustained capacity or project based support broader usefulness increases the credibility of later grant language the pi or lab base is the first relationship the administrative and academic base the structure that makes outward facing collaboration easier it should not be understood as the final limit of the work why this is low risk and high upside this is a low risk first step because the immediate ask is small the practical value is already clear multiple continuity paths can grow around the base the lab gains useful capacity right away it is high upside because sustained capacity becomes more plausible grant writing becomes more credible cross campus collaboration becomes easier sutton signwriting infrastructure becomes structurally possible this is exactly the kind of situation where a modest first institutional decision can unlock a much larger set of outcomes later recommended framing for conversation the clearest framing for a pi conversation is this is a practical first move the work already has immediate utility the base lowers friction for everything that follows outside support can grow around the pi or lab base the pi does not need to solve the entire future on day one the order matters lead with place steve now let useful work begin and let the structure grow from demonstrated value immediate ask the immediate ask is straightforward give steve slevinski a pi or lab base now let practical dms plan workflow implementation repository collaboration and grant activity grow around that base let support grow from demonstrated value everything else becomes easier once that first step is real closing the main question is not whether the entire future infrastructure is already funded the main question is whether the university can support the right first structure now if there is a pi or lab base now then the work has a real place to start sustained capacity becomes easier to build dms plan workflow and repository collaborations become easier to activate grant writing becomes more credible the broader sutton signwriting infrastructure program becomes more realistic that is why the pi or lab base matters so much it is not the end goal it is the first move that allows the rest of the strategy to work

records.title: Plane-Based Writing and Script Typology Paper
records.summary: Paper for Plane-Based Writing and Script Typology. SignWriting is best understood through a two-axis typology: what writing represents and how the written unit is organized for reading. Where SignWriting fits, and where existing writing-system categories strain
keywords: Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting
records.contentExcerpt: Plane-Based Writing and Script Typology Where SignWriting fits, and where existing writing-system categories strain This paper carries the typological claim for Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting: SignWriting requires writing-system analysis to ask not only what wri...
records.searchText: plane based writing and script typology paper paper for plane based writing and script typology signwriting is best understood through a two axis typology what writing represents and how the written unit is organized for reading where signwriting fits and where existing writing system categories strain series writing and representation plane based writing and script typology primary writing and representation in sutton signwriting plane based writing and script typology paper theoretical paper working paper plane based writing and script typology plane based writing and script typology where signwriting fits and where existing writing system categories strain this paper carries the typological claim for writing and representation in sutton signwriting signwriting requires writing system analysis to ask not only what writing represents but how the primary written unit is organized for reading the signed writing system considered here is sutton signwriting it is a strong case of plane based writing because writers select symbols and compose them spatially inside a written sign existing typologies remain useful they give the field important vocabulary for alphabetic syllabic morphographic featural notational and mixed systems signwriting exposes a limit in the usual question because much of its written structure appears inside a composed spatial unit rather than only in a line of ordered marks the central question is therefore if writing does not have to be linear how should a plane based signed writing system be placed in script typology the typology problem a writing system can be difficult to classify for two very different reasons one reason is weakness in the system itself the system may lack stable signs consistent conventions teachable structure public use or durable infrastructure another reason is weakness in the classification question the system may be doing something real but the available categories may have been shaped around a narrower sample of writing traditions signwriting creates this second kind of problem it is not hard to classify because it lacks written structure it is hard to classify because much of its written structure appears where many typologies have not placed their main attention inside a composed spatial unit a written sign is not merely a mark for a sound a syllable a word or a morpheme it is also a structured written object the writer selects symbols and arranges them inside a signbox that arrangement is not decorative typography added after the writing has already happened it is part of the written form this creates a typological pressure point if the classification of writing systems only asks does this system represent speech sounds does it represent syllables does it represent morphemes does it represent words does it represent meaning then signed writing will be pulled toward categories that do not fully describe what it is doing a better question has two parts what does the writing represent how is the written unit organized signwriting needs both questions existing typology still matters this paper does not reject writing system typology it depends on it work associated with grammatology script typology notation theory and grapholinguistics has made several distinctions that remain useful here daniels and bright give writing system study a wide comparative base across historical and contemporary scripts coulmas and sampson help keep the relation between writing language sound meaning and script type analytically visible goodman s work on notation is useful because it asks when a visible mark system becomes sufficiently articulated repeatable and shareable to support stable reference meletis and d rscheid s grapholinguistic account is especially important because it treats writing as a structured object of study rather than as a mere shadow of speech while also showing how difficult typology remains the present paper builds from that literature rather than setting it aside the useful inherited distinctions include writing is not identical to speech scripts can be classified by their relation to linguistic units many writing systems are mixed rather than pure written form has its own internal structure materiality layout and use matter typologies are tools not final truths those distinctions matter because they prevent two bad shortcuts the first shortcut is to say signwriting is visual so it is not really writing the second shortcut is to say signwriting is writing so it must fit neatly into an existing speech shaped category both shortcuts fail the first denies the writing before studying it the second accepts the writing but misdescribes it a stronger account should preserve what existing typology already does well while adding an axis that signed writing makes necessary that addition should be understood as an extension not a rejection the claim is not typology has failed because signwriting exists the claim is typology becomes more accurate when it asks not only what writing represents but also how written units are organized that does not mean every signwriting question is already settled adoption scale handwriting practice literacy outcomes institutional continuity and standards interoperability remain evidence and infrastructure questions for other parts of the platform the narrower claim here is that existing typology needs an organizational axis to describe what signwriting already does structurally the missing axis organization many familiar classifications focus on representational correspondence they ask what kind of linguistic unit is associated with written marks common typological question examples of answers what does the script mainly represent phonemes syllables morphemes words semantic units features how directly does writing map to language shallow deep mixed morphophonemic logographic phonographic what kind of sign inventory is used alphabet abjad abugida syllabary character system notation system those questions are useful they are not enough a writing system also has an organizational basis that basis asks how written units are arranged into readable form organizational question examples of answers is the primary written unit a point line block cluster plane staff diagram or page alphabetic words chinese characters mathematical formulas musical staffs signwriting signboxes does placement belong to typography or to the authored written unit line breaking in prose versus symbol placement inside a signbox is spatial relation recoverable from the written text yes in plane based composition often no in a simple list of symbols does reading depend mainly on sequence grouping spatial relation or some mixture sequence dominant scripts block systems formulaic notation spatial signed writing this organizational axis is not a replacement for representational typology it is an additional axis for many alphabetic scripts the representational and organizational answers seem to move together the script is phonographic the text is mostly linear the encoded order often resembles the reading order that combination is common enough that it can start to look natural or universal it is not universal some writing and notation systems already complicate it mathematical notation uses superscripts fractions radicals matrices diagrams and spatial alignment musical notation organizes pitch duration voice staff position measure and performance instruction across a visual field chinese characters can be composed internally as square graphic units while also participating in line based text manuscript layout tables diagrams and scientific notation all show that written meaning can depend on more than sequence signwriting makes this organizational question primary rather than occasional that difference is important mathematics and music show that spatial organization can carry disciplined meaning chinese character structure shows that written units can have internal graphic organization while also participating in line based text tables diagrams and manuscript layout show that page structure can guide reading but these examples do not automatically become the same kind of object as signwriting they are boundary tests they show that sequence is not the only organizing principle available to writing and notation while also forcing a sharper question when does spatial organization belong to the language writing unit itself rather than to a formula score diagram page design or secondary layout for signwriting the answer is unusually direct spatial organization belongs inside the written sign that is why the signbox is typologically relevant defining plane based writing this paper uses plane based writing as an analytic descriptor it is not presented as an established field category it is a proposed way to name what signwriting makes visible definition plane based writing is writing in which the primary written unit is composed through spatial relation within a two dimensional field and where that spatial relation is part of the authored written form rather than only a downstream layout effect this definition has several important limits it does not mean that every mark placed on a page is plane based writing all writing appears somewhere alphabetic text is printed on a page but the page location of each letter is usually not part of the authored structure of the word it is mostly a consequence of typography line breaking font metrics and layout plane based writing is different in plane based writing internal placement belongs to the written unit itself for signwriting this means the signbox matters the signbox is not merely a bounding rectangle around a picture it is the written field in which symbols are selected placed oriented and related the spatial arrangement is part of the sign s written identity this also does not mean that plane based writing is unconstrained a plane is not a free for all plane based writing still depends on stable symbols learned conventions recurring graphic relations teachable structure reader expectations boundaries around written units ways to preserve render and compare written forms in signwriting the plane is authored it is not accidental background it is also not the whole page the primary plane based unit in signwriting is the written sign inside its signbox passage layout then arranges those written signs into lines columns lanes and pages that distinction prevents another possible confusion plane based writing does not mean that every page level layout choice is part of each written sign it means the internal spatial composition of the written sign is part of the writing system a two axis typology the most useful move is to separate two questions axis 1 what kind of language or symbolic structure does the writing preserve axis 2 how is the written unit organized for reading this creates a more flexible typology system or notation type representational basis organizational basis alphabetic writing mostly phonemic or segmental mostly linear sequence abjads and abugidas consonantal or consonant vowel structure mostly linear sequence with internal mark relations syllabaries syllables mostly linear sequence chinese characters morphosyllabic and lexical structure character block plus line based text mathematical notation symbolic relational quantitative structure line stack plane diagram and page musical notation pitch duration rhythm performance instruction staff based spatial field over time linguistic transcription analytic linguistic categories usually linear with diacritics and grouping hamnosys analytic sign form notation mainly sequence oriented notation signwriting articulatory spatial signed form as written language signbox based plane composition plus passage layout this table is not meant to erase differences among these systems it is meant to show why one axis is not enough it also avoids a false choice signwriting can be partly featural in what its symbols preserve partly iconic in the motivation of some forms partly notational in some analytic uses and still be plane based in the organization of its primary written unit those descriptions answer different questions they should not be forced to compete as if only one label can be true signwriting is not simply another symbol inventory to be inserted into a linear script typology it is a system in which the organization of the primary written unit is itself typologically important why signwriting is not merely pictorial the most common misunderstanding is to treat signwriting as drawing that mistake is understandable signwriting looks visually motivated it uses symbols for hands movement facial expression body location and spatial relation a reader can often see why a symbol has the shape it has but visual motivation does not make something non writing many writing systems contain motivated forms historic iconicity graphic analogy or visible relation between form and meaning the question is not whether a form is visually motivated the question is whether it is conventionalized enough to function as writing signwriting is not free drawing because it uses a stable symbol inventory defined symbol classes learned orientation and fill distinctions recurring spatial conventions sign boundaries passage layout practices computational encodings that preserve written structure a drawing can be interpreted a written sign can be read copied encoded searched corrected compared and taught as part of a shared system the difference is not that writing lacks visual resemblance the difference is that writing stabilizes resemblance through convention that is the bridge to iconicity convention and legibility why signwriting is not merely featural another near miss is to call signwriting a featural script there is something useful in that instinct signwriting does represent internal features of signed articulation handshape palm facing orientation location movement facial and body information but featural is not enough a list of features is not yet a written sign the sign is composed the writer does not merely say that a sign contains a handshape a movement and a facial expression the writer arranges selected symbols in a spatial field so the reader can recover a written form that means signwriting has featural elements but its primary written unit is not simply a feature bundle it is a plane composed written sign this distinction matters because it prevents a common reduction if the symbols name articulatory features then the writing system is just a feature notation no the symbols matter but the composed signbox matters too the typological description must account for both why signwriting is not merely notation signwriting can be used analytically it can support dictionaries corpora search comparison and linguistic study but that does not make it only notation notation systems often serve specialists who need precise controlled representations for analysis writing systems can also be used by ordinary readers and writers to produce durable texts the boundary is not always sharp musical notation mathematical notation linguistic transcription and dance notation all show that notation and writing overlap in complex ways the important question is functional is the system only a specialist analytic code or can it serve as readable written language signwriting is designed and used as readable signed text not only as a researcher s transcription system the evidence for public use educational use and institutional visibility is not developed in this paper that evidence belongs in sutton signwriting evidence and adoption especially the entries on brazil south korea and the current evidence base the point here is typological once a system is intended and used as readable signed text it should not be reduced to specialist notation merely because it can also support analysis that is why the typological account should not stop at notation it should describe a writing system that also supports notational computational educational and analytic work why signwriting is not merely encoding a third mistake appears when signwriting enters software because formal signwriting fsw swu fonts svg and unicode are technically complex readers may mistake the machine layer for the writing system that reverses the order the writing comes first the encoding preserves and processes the writing formal signwriting is important because it gives the authored written form a durable technical model a temporal prefix where sorting sequence can be stored a spatial signbox where the written composition is preserved symbol identity coordinates sign boundaries conversion between fsw and swu rendering paths search and comparison support but the existence of an encoding does not mean the writing has become a line based code serialization is a machine requirement plane based composition is a writing system property that distinction is developed later in this series especially in human writing and machine encoding what signwriting contributes to typology signwriting contributes three important pressures to writing system typology first it shows that modality matters signed languages are not spoken languages transferred into the hands they are natural languages in a visual spatial modality a writing system for signed language should not be expected to look like a writing system for speech second it shows that organization matters if a writing system preserves spatially organized signed form then the written unit may need to be spatially organized too sequence alone is not the only possible organizing principle for writing third it shows that authored relation matters in signwriting placement is not always a typographic afterthought placement can be part of the written sign that is what turns the signbox into a typological issue rather than a software convenience together these pressures suggest that writing system typology should distinguish representational type organizational type material and rendering type use type encoding and infrastructure type those layers interact but they are not the same this is where signwriting differs from a merely exotic example it does not only add another case to an existing list of scripts it asks whether the list itself has been organized around scripts whose main written units are easier to describe as sequences if so signed writing is not a marginal exception it is a diagnostic case it shows where the typology needs another dimension a typological description of signwriting within this series a careful description of signwriting would be signwriting is a plane based writing system for signed languages whose written signs are composed from a stable symbol inventory within bounded signboxes and arranged into passages through learned layout conventions that sentence is deliberately layered signwriting identifies the tradition being described in this series plane based writing system identifies the organizational claim for signed languages identifies the language modality written signs identifies the primary written unit stable symbol inventory identifies the alphabet layer bounded signboxes identifies the internal field of composition passages and layout conventions identify how signs move beyond isolated units into text no single inherited label does all of that work that is why the description has to be built what this paper claims this paper makes five claims first signwriting should be treated as writing not as illustration second its typological description requires both a representational axis and an organizational axis third plane based writing is a useful analytic descriptor for systems whose primary written units are composed through authored spatial relation fourth the signbox is typologically significant because it is where written spatial composition is authored and preserved fifth existing writing system typologies should be extended rather than discarded the goal is not to replace alphabetic syllabic morphographic featural or notational categories the goal is to avoid forcing signed writing into categories that were not built to see it clearly scope and next questions plane based writing is a working analytic term offered for scrutiny and refinement its job is not to rank writing systems or make signwriting the required model for all signed writing its job is to make fair comparison possible by naming a structural fact in sutton signwriting authored spatial relation belongs to the written unit that claim leaves several questions for neighboring documents reading fluency educational effects adoption patterns and community use belong primarily in sutton signwriting language and literacy research and sutton signwriting evidence and adoption comparative script design belongs in other sign language scripts and their conceptions technical preservation of the signbox belongs in formal signwriting future work may refine the two axis account and the terms used here why this belongs first in the writing systems series this paper belongs near the front of writing and representation in sutton signwriting because it changes the question the rest of the series answers without this paper the series can seem to be saying signwriting is unusual and here are some unusual properties with this paper the series can say something stronger signwriting exposes a missing organizational axis in writing system description that does not finish the argument it starts it properly the following entries then have clearer jobs perspective and authored space explains what the writer authors inside the plane iconicity convention and legibility explains how motivated visual form becomes disciplined writing handwriting and spatial script practice explains how a spatial script lives in human material practice human writing and machine encoding explains how a plane composed written unit can become machine processable text without becoming line based writing the shape of a sign in formal signwriting formalizes the signbox symbol identity coordinates and boundaries other sign language scripts and their conceptions compares neighboring script conceptions without making them carry the main typological argument conclusion signwriting does not ask writing system typology to abandon its existing categories it asks typology to add a question that should have been explicit all along how is the written unit organized for many scripts the answer is mostly linear sequence for signwriting the answer is different the written sign is composed in a plane that does not make it less like writing it makes visible a dimension of writing that line based systems often allow us to ignore plane based writing is therefore not a slogan it is a proposed typological descriptor for a real structural fact in signwriting authored spatial relation belongs to the written form itself that is the point from which the rest of the writing systems series can proceed selected references coulmas f 1989 the writing systems of the world blackwell daniels p t bright w eds 1996 the world s writing systems oxford university press goodman n 1976 languages of art an approach to a theory of symbols 2nd ed hackett meletis d d rscheid c 2022 writing systems and their use an overview of grapholinguistics de gruyter sampson g 1985 writing systems a linguistic introduction stanford university press slevinski s 2026 human writing and machine encoding authorship serialization and the layers of signed text in writing and representation in sutton signwriting slevinski s 2026 perspective and authored space viewpoint placement and the authored space of signed writing in writing and representation in sutton signwriting sutton v 1995 lessons in signwriting deaf action committee for signwriting sutton v 2010 iswa 2010 international signwriting alphabet center for sutton movement writing

records.title: Reading Signed Text Paper
records.summary: Paper for Reading Signed Text. Recognition, syntax, spatial reference, layout direction, and evidence limits shape the research question of reading signed text. Recognition, syntax, and spatial reference in signed-language literacy
keywords: Sutton SignWriting Language and Literacy Research
records.contentExcerpt: Reading Signed Text Recognition, syntax, and spatial reference in signed-language literacy This paper asks the next research-facing question after The Four Language Pathways: if signed language gains durable written form, what would reading mean in that system? That question matt...
records.searchText: reading signed text paper paper for reading signed text recognition syntax spatial reference layout direction and evidence limits shape the research question of reading signed text recognition syntax and spatial reference in signed language literacy series language and literacy research reading signed text primary sutton signwriting language and literacy research reading signed text paper theoretical paper working paper reading signed text reading signed text recognition syntax and spatial reference in signed language literacy this paper asks the next research facing question after the four language pathways if signed language gains durable written form what would reading mean in that system that question matters because a research series cannot stop at the claim that signed language can be written it has to say more carefully what becomes legible what kinds of recognition problems emerge how syntax and spatial reference might survive into text and which questions remain open this paper does not pretend to resolve all of that its narrower purpose is to define the reading problem clearly enough that the research space stops sounding vague terminology note this paper uses reading pathways in a narrower sense than the developmental pathway model the four language pathways used pathways for developmental routes into language and literacy this paper is narrower it asks how signed text becomes readable how written signs are perceived how they are recognized as units how they are compared revisited and parsed how they reach language interpretation during reading why this paper comes next the four language pathways established a developmental distinction language comes first literacy comes second literacy input and literacy output pathways matter that model opens the research series the next step is to ask what the literacy layer would actually require once it exists for psycholinguistics literacy research and sign language research that means moving from can signed language have durable written pathways to what would readers do with those reading pathways once the literacy layer exists that is the problem of reading signed text reading is not only decoding many discussions of writing still begin from alphabetic decoding that is useful in some contexts but it is too narrow as a general model of literacy readers do more than convert one stream into another they recognize recurring forms compare neighboring forms recover structure revisit earlier material build expectations across text use durable symbols as reusable objects those operations matter in any writing system the question here is how they would operate in a visual spatial writing system for signed language that does not mean existing reading science is irrelevant it means the starting question has to be adjusted the simple view of reading helps distinguish text recognition from language comprehension dual route and word recognition models help explain how printed words may be processed in familiar writing systems eye movement research gives tools for studying fixation regressions rereading and layout effects signed text can use those tools but it cannot simply inherit all of their assumptions the unit being recognized may be a written sign rather than a printed word spatial composition may be part of the written form rather than surrounding layout reading may involve symbol recognition signbox structure viewpoint and syntactic reference in ways that do not map cleanly onto alphabetic decoding recognition in signed text the first reading question is recognition what would it mean to recognize a written sign a reader is not only following a sequence of letters a reader is encountering a bounded written object that preserves symbol identity relative placement grouped composition authored viewpoint that means recognition is not exhausted by symbol naming it involves recovering a composed sign as a unit this is one reason signwriting should not be treated as merely an unusual display of decomposed features the written sign is not only a list it is a structured visual object that suggests at least three reading questions unit recognition how quickly and reliably do readers recognize a written sign as a whole near neighbor competition how do visually similar written signs compete with one another during reading internal structure which aspects of the sign are treated as most informative for fast recognition handshape movement placement facial markers overall spatial composition these are ordinary reading questions in a new writing environment reading and comparison durable text changes more than one shot recognition it also changes comparison readers can compare one form with another compare one occurrence with another compare related signs across a lexicon or corpus revise and revisit the same written material this matters because comparison is part of what makes literacy powerful video can preserve performance well but writable text makes side by side inspection repeated lookup annotation and structured reuse much easier to routinize so the reading problem is not only can the text be read it is also what kinds of comparison become normal once signed language is available as durable text that is part of why written sign matters scientifically layout as a reading variable reading signed text is not only a question of symbol recognition inside an isolated sign passage layout may also affect the reading pathway this is visible in the history of signwriting layout early text was written horizontally valerie sutton later documented the vertical shift as a deaf user driven change deaf staff members lucinda o grady batch george butch zein kathy say bonita ewan and kevin clark expressed interest in writing down the page in columns and sutton officially announced the switch to vertical writing for dac publications in january 1997 sutton also described the same practical history on the signwriting list in 2004 deaf native signing staff who had written signwriting by hand in the 1980s told her they wanted to write vertically the historical point is therefore well documented the reading speed point is more modest after the change a small group of deaf staff and readers reported that vertical writing felt easier and faster that history is not a large scale experimental result it is a small self reported design observation from roughly five people who were directly affected by the change even so it matters for research framing it suggests that layout direction may change how readers maintain orientation to the signed body left right relations and the composed space of the sign while moving through a passage vertical writing may reduce the need to reorient each sign as a separate object in a horizontal row the research point is modest layout direction should be treated as a reading variable that does not settle the processing question it makes the question more precise future studies should be able to compare horizontal and vertical passages using the same signed content with measures such as reader preference comprehension rereading behavior speed and eye movement patterns early study dimensions the reading questions become more useful when they are tied to possible tasks and measures the following table is not a fixed protocol it is a first operational map for turning the reading agenda into studies near neighbor competition means that written signs sharing handshape location movement or spatial configuration features may compete during recognition in ways analogous to neighborhood effects in print reading research area early task possible measures unit recognition identify isolated written signs under controlled variation accuracy latency confidence similarity and near neighbor competition compare visually similar written signs confusion rates similarity ratings priming effects internal structure read partial masked or feature altered written signs feature deletion effects recognition thresholds error patterns layout direction read the same signed passage vertically and horizontally comprehension reading time rereading eye movements preference syntax and reference resolve written spatial reference in short passages interpretation accuracy explanation quality recall text reuse annotate revise or compare written signed texts revision quality navigation behavior annotation patterns syntax in written signed language one of the hardest questions follows from signed language structure itself written spoken languages often map temporal order into spatial order fairly directly but signed languages may also use space itself for reference and structure that means syntax cannot be treated as an afterthought a serious research series has to ask how much structure remains sequential in reading how much structure is carried by the bounded written sign how much structure is distributed across layout grouping or convention the key point is not that signed language lacks syntax the key point is that written signed language may not externalize syntax in exactly the same way alphabetic print often does sign language linguistics already treats spatial grammar reference classifier constructions role shift and viewpoint as structured linguistic phenomena the reading question is how those structures are preserved conventionalized or redistributed when signing becomes durable text that is a research problem not a reason to dismiss the writing spatial reference and anaphora a related issue concerns spatial reference signed languages often use space for reference tracking role relations anaphora viewpoint structure the question for literacy is not whether this disappears in writing the question is how it is preserved normalized reduced made conventional or redistributed across other textual cues this is exactly where reading research and writing systems research need one another some questions are about language processing others are about representational convention the current research series should make both visible what current evidence supports current evidence supports several starting claims signed language can have durable written form signwriting preserves meaningful written structure rather than only glossing spoken language small reader reported design histories suggest that layout direction affects ease of reading real readers and institutions already exist especially in brazil current institutional case studies exist including south korea the next research layer should measure exactly which recognition mechanisms dominate skilled reading exactly how much vertical layout changes speed comprehension or processing load exactly how syntax and spatial reference are processed in all reading contexts exactly which text forms are easiest or hardest for different reader populations the current task is to frame the research question well enough that those experiments become easier to design a research program that follows from this once the reading problem is stated more clearly the next studies become easier to imagine lexical recognition studies sign identification under controlled variation similarity and confusion patterns repeated exposure and fluency effects short passage reading studies comprehension of short written passages navigation and revisiting behavior annotation and recall patterns horizontal versus vertical passage comparison syntax and reference studies how readers resolve reference in written signed text how viewpoint and spatial conventions affect interpretation how layout interacts with clause or discourse structure corpus and materials work building readable text sets for experiments collecting normalized passages and metadata developing comparison tasks and reusable stimuli these are not fantasy studies they become realistic because the platform has real texts real users real empirical anchors and a growing technical base why this paper matters this paper matters because it narrows the research series to something more recognizable it says that the hard science question is not whether language can exist without sound the harder scientific questions are what recognition looks like in signed text what comparison and reuse look like in signed text how syntax and spatial reference survive in written form what new reading research becomes possible once the text exists that is a more useful research center conclusion signed language literacy is not only the existence of a writing system it is the emergence of a new reading problem that problem includes recognition comparison syntax spatial reference durable textual reuse those questions are not yet fully answered but they are clear enough to define a real research program that is the contribution of this paper it turns a general literacy claim into a more precise reading agenda the paper s strongest claim is therefore not that signwriting reading has already been explained it is that signed language writing makes a new class of reading questions experimentally visible how readers recognize written signs as composed units how they navigate spatially organized signs and passages how they use durable signed text for comparison and reuse how syntax and spatial reference are preserved conventionalized or redistributed in written form those are not side questions after adoption they are the research problem that adoption makes possible the strongest next move after this paper is research program for literacy without sound which asks how those questions can be studied with real texts real users and real infrastructure selected references coltheart m rastle k perry c langdon r ziegler j 2001 drc a dual route cascaded model of visual word recognition and reading aloud psychological review 108 1 204 256 dehaene s 2009 reading in the brain the new science of how we read viking emmorey k weisberg j mccullough s petrich j a f 2013 mapping the reading circuitry for skilled deaf readers an fmri study of semantic and phonological processing brain and language 126 2 169 180 gough p b tunmer w e 1986 decoding reading and reading disability remedial and special education 7 1 6 10 hoover w a gough p b 1990 the simple view of reading reading and writing 2 127 160 hou j rashid j lee k m 2017 cognitive map or medium materiality reading on paper and screen computers in human behavior 67 84 94 rayner k 1998 eye movements in reading and information processing 20 years of research psychological bulletin 124 3 372 422 reich l szwed m cohen l amedi a 2011 a ventral visual stream reading center independent of visual experience current biology 21 5 363 368 sutton v 1997 signwriting 1996 1997 writing went south the vertical evolution signwriting org sutton v 2004 june 19 typing signwriting in vertical columns signwriting list trasmundi s b van den heuvel e koppen m steffensen s v 2022 text materialities affordances and the embodied turn in the study of reading frontiers in psychology 13 827058 wolf m 2007 proust and the squid the story and science of the reading brain harper

records.title: Relevant Precedents for SignWriting Infrastructure Brief
records.summary: Brief for Relevant Precedents for SignWriting Infrastructure. SignWriting follows a recognizable infrastructure path: mature assets first, institutional anchoring second, staffing and continuity next, and grants or philanthropy after that. What comparable archive, language, data, and research-infrastructure models suggest
records.contentExcerpt: Relevant Precedents for SignWriting Infrastructure What comparable archive, language, data, and research-infrastructure models suggest Sutton SignWriting is unusual. The stewardship path it needs is not. Across language documentation, social-science data, sign-language corpora, a...
records.searchText: relevant precedents for signwriting infrastructure brief brief for relevant precedents for signwriting infrastructure signwriting follows a recognizable infrastructure path mature assets first institutional anchoring second staffing and continuity next and grants or philanthropy after that what comparable archive language data and research infrastructure models suggest series infrastructure and program relevant precedents for signwriting infrastructure primary sutton signwriting infrastructure and program relevant precedents for signwriting infrastructure brief brief program brief relevant precedents for signwriting infrastructure relevant precedents for signwriting infrastructure what comparable archive language data and research infrastructure models suggest sutton signwriting is unusual the stewardship path it needs is not across language documentation social science data sign language corpora and research infrastructure the same general pattern appears repeatedly mature assets first institutional anchoring second staffing and credibility next grants philanthropy and longer term infrastructure growth after that this entry uses precedent as comparison not equivalence the infrastructure move is recognizable the examples below are drawn mostly from english language institutional records in the united states united kingdom and australia they are useful analogs not a claim that stewardship must follow an anglo american model brazilian korean and other national research or public infrastructure contexts may offer additional paths why precedents matter precedents clarify two important points first signwriting does not need an institution to create its value the value already exists in the writing system tools standards data public resources and documented use second institutional stewardship around an already developed language infrastructure is not strange or unprecedented many important infrastructure efforts begin when existing assets become too valuable too public or too complex to remain dependent on informal stewardship signwriting fits that pattern it already has working data tools standards technical stewardship real community educational and research use what it needs next is stronger continuity staffing documentation and host structure closest analogs these examples are useful because they show language or data infrastructure becoming more durable through institutional anchoring icpsr university of michigan founding year 1962 how it started political scientist warren e miller created the inter university consortium for political and social research at the university of michigan s institute for social research as the data dissemination arm of the american national election studies at a time when important datasets were often closely held by individual researchers funding model it began with membership dues from participating universities and later expanded through foundation support federal support and consortial membership what it provides long term preservation curation documentation training researcher services and public access to a large social science data archive why relevant to signwriting icpsr shows how a university can give durable form to a shared data need that already exists then grow it into recognized research infrastructure source trail icpsr s history page describes the 1962 founding by warren e miller to share scientific data icpsr s early years account describes the american national election studies connection and the early consortial dues model including the 21 additional universities recruited by the end of 1962 see icpsr history and the founding and early years eldp elar founding year 2002 how it started the endangered languages documentation programme and endangered languages archive were launched through philanthropic support to soas and a model in which funded documentation work became part of an archival program funding model philanthropic seed funding annual documentation grants and sustained institutional hosting what it provides grants for language documentation plus an archive for preserving and sharing documentation materials why relevant to signwriting eldp and elar show that philanthropy can be a strong continuity pathway when paired with real institutional anchoring and clear archival responsibility source trail eldp states that it was founded in 2002 by the arcadia fund and provides annual documentation grants bbaw describes elar as a digital archive launched at soas in 2002 soas documents the 2021 move of eldp to berlin see eldp who we are bbaw elar and soas on the eldp move paradisec founding period 2003 with consortium development shortly afterward how it started faculty at multiple australian universities recognized that endangered language and cultural recordings were at risk and built a shared preservation and access model funding model distributed university support research infrastructure grants and shared operational responsibility what it provides long term archiving cataloguing digitization preservation and community access for endangered language and cultural recordings why relevant to signwriting paradisec shows that a durable stewardship structure can be distributed rather than centralized as long as preservation and access are treated as infrastructure functions source trail paradisec describes itself as a digital archive for records of small cultures and languages with models for community access and digital archiving standards its funding page documents support from universities the australian research council and related sources its presentation history documents paradisec activity in 2003 see paradisec about us paradisec funding and paradisec presentations bsl corpus project to bsl signbank founding period the bsl corpus project ran from 2008 to 2011 with bsl signbank development continuing afterward how it started an esrc funded sign language documentation effort at university college london s deafness cognition and language research centre produced a large corpus that fed into reusable lexical infrastructure funding model research council funding followed by continued university hosting and stewardship what it provides a large video corpus together with a public lexical resource used for research teaching and community access why relevant to signwriting this is one of the closest sign language precedents because it shows sign language documentation maturing into durable public lexical infrastructure source trail the bsl corpus project states that it was an esrc funded project led by ucl s deafness cognition and language research centre initially 2008 2010 and extended to 2011 bsl signbank identifies the bsl corpus as one of its two primary sources see bsl corpus project about and bsl signbank dictionary adjacent but useful analogs these examples are not perfect structural matches but they strengthen important parts of the infrastructure case asllrp asl signbank asllvd founding period late 1990s and early 2000s with continued expansion how it started linguistic research at boston university collected and annotated asl video data that developed into public tools and lexical resources funding model research grants including nsf supported work documented by asllrp plus university based stewardship what it provides video corpora lexical resources search interfaces and downloadable annotations why relevant to signwriting this shows that sign language infrastructure naturally converges around corpora lexical databases annotation search and tools source trail asllrp describes nsf supported work on asl linguistic structure computer based recognition generation multimedia tools public corpora signstream dai and the asllrp asl sign bank boston university s asllvd page describes the asl lexicon video dataset as more than 3 300 asl signs and almost 9 800 tokens see asllrp asllrp resources and dai asllvd midas university of michigan founding period around 2015 with its 10th anniversary marked in 2025 how it started a cross campus institute was created to advance data science and ai as shared research infrastructure across many disciplines funding model university support plus pilot grants research services proposal support and partnership development what it provides research resources training pilot funding proposal support collaborator matching partnership development and cross campus research coordination why relevant to signwriting midas is useful as a model for shared technical capacity practical support can create immediate value while also generating collaborations and proposal leverage source trail midas describes itself as a university wide organization that advances data science and ai provides research resources training postdoctoral programs and collaborations university of michigan s technology timeline records midas as created in july 2015 as part of the data science initiative midas grant materials describe proposal support collaborator identification and research synergy see midas u m accessible it timeline and midas pods rfp pattern across the examples the examples differ but the pattern is stable pattern element what it means for signwriting existing assets first signwriting already has standards software data public resources and use institutional anchoring second a host gives the work credibility continuity and administrative pathways staffing and continuity next technical data translation and coordination work become visible responsibilities grants and philanthropy after that funding becomes more plausible when the infrastructure has a credible host structure public access and preservation throughout the goal is not only research production but durable access and reuse this pattern is important because it keeps the infrastructure case grounded the public case is not that signwriting might someday become useful the stronger case is that signwriting is already useful enough for informal maintenance to become the weak point how to use these precedents these precedents are supporting evidence not the first argument use them when a reader asks is there a real model for this kind of move the answer is yes the models differ but they all show that language and data infrastructure often becomes durable only after an institution consortium archive or program recognizes that an existing asset base needs a stronger stewardship structure reusable sentences sutton signwriting is not a speculative new project it is mature infrastructure with domains tools standards libraries and datasets already in active use icpsr shows how a university housed data need can become recognized shared research infrastructure once curation access training and continuity are supported the bsl corpus to bsl signbank path shows how sign language documentation can mature into durable public lexical infrastructure eldp elar and paradisec show that philanthropic and distributed institutional models can support durable language infrastructure when anchored in real organizational structures rather than improvised continuity arrangements midas shows that shared technical capacity can produce immediate institutional value while also supporting longer term research growth bottom line signwriting does not need an institution to invent its value it needs a host structure capable of recognizing preserving staffing and extending value that already exists that is a familiar infrastructure move the unusual part is not the path the unusual part is that so much of the technical and community asset base already exists while the durable stewardship structure is still catching up

Its core claim is simple: rendering is downstream from canonical text Minimal vocabulary The rendering layer d...
records.searchText: rendering formal signwriting technical paper technical paper for rendering formal signwriting svg fonts layout and output models belong to the rendering layer rather than canonical text svg fonts layout and output models series formal signwriting rendering formal signwriting primary formal signwriting rendering formal signwriting technical paper technical paper technical paper rendering formal signwriting rendering formal signwriting svg fonts layout and output models formal signwriting is easier to implement when the rendering layer stays distinct from canonical text its core claim is simple rendering is downstream from canonical text minimal vocabulary the rendering layer depends on a small vocabulary term meaning canonical text the durable encoded source rendering a visible realization of that source font based svg rendering that depends on signwriting fonts stand alone svg rendering assembled from graphic resources rather than installed fonts styling optional customization applied downstream from canonical text if those boundaries stay clear the rendering layer stays clear canonical text versus presentation one distinction governs the whole layer canonical text is the durable technical representation rendering is a chosen visible realization of that representation if the text model stays stable multiple rendering strategies can coexist without breaking the architecture rendering as a technical layer rendering is where many people first encounter formal signwriting in practice they see an svg image a font based display a web component a printed page but those visible outputs are only the surface of a deeper system a dedicated rendering discussion makes it possible to explain how those outputs are generated without confusing them with encoding or grammar a minimal rendering pipeline the rendering pipeline can be summarized this way that sequence matters because it keeps the encoded text stable while allowing different output models svg and fonts svg and font based output remain central implementation pathways they matter because they provide scalable visible output copyable technical text portable display in web environments a bridge between symbolic data and readable writing this is one place where formal signwriting shows its maturity as a technical system it can be rendered in more than one way while preserving a stable underlying representation a compact svg example a sign such as can produce an svg header like and symbol placement such as this is a good example because it shows that rendering is not abstract it is a practical repeatable transformation from encoded text into visible structure many outputs also preserve hidden copyable text inside the svg that keeps visible graphics and encoded text together in one output artifact this is a rendering profile choice rather than a requirement of canonical text it is useful for copyable or archival svg output but a deployment may omit hidden text for accessibility size security or host format reasons if the canonical text is preserved elsewhere stand alone graphics font based rendering is not the only pathway stand alone svg assembled from graphic resources is also important because it avoids dependence on installed fonts and can support server side generation asset pipelines and archival output that makes rendering more durable across software environments font loading a browser facing rendering path often depends on explicit font loading the same pattern applies to suttonsignwritingfill suttonsignwritingoned these fonts do not do the same job font primary use baseline and color behavior suttonsignwritingline line layer for font backed svg and graphics descends below the baseline and aligns with the svg oriented symbol model suttonsignwritingfill fill layer for two color output pairs with line for svg oriented output and second color fills suttonsignwritingoned one dimensional text editor display aligns for text editing rather than svg composition and does not carry the second color this distinction matters because font choice is not only aesthetic it affects baseline behavior svg compatibility and whether two color output is available layout and lanes rendering becomes more interesting when signs are not isolated a dedicated layout entry defines vertical writing horizontal writing lane counts centering strategy and sw1 sw4 reading level defaults rendering should use that layout model rather than redefining it inside the output layer a real written passage requires vertical writing flow lane aware alignment sign sizing centered layout behavior html and css coordination this is why rendering cannot be reduced to drawing one sign at a time the architecture has to support page behavior and text flow as well at the document level the classic browser layout pattern includes writing mode vertical lr per sign containers with explicit width and height lane offsets through margin or transparent border rules print behavior that preserves column flow mixed language environments need their own host layout rules signwriting containers can use vertical layout while surrounding spoken language text captions metadata or interface text use a different writing mode or direction the renderer should keep those concerns explicit rather than letting one css writing mode silently redefine the other fonts as implementation infrastructure the rendering layer is also where font resources become part of the technical stack common implementation pathways include suttonsignwritingline suttonsignwritingfill suttonsignwritingoned these are not the model itself but they are important pieces of how the model becomes usable in browsers and applications the practical guidance is use line and fill together when the goal is svg oriented two color visual output use oned when the goal is compact text editor display or one dimensional inspection do not treat a font s baseline behavior as part of canonical sign text document which rendering profile a system uses when output alignment matters styling is related but separate rendering and styling are closely related but they are not the same topic rendering explains output models styling explains customization semantics rendering can reference styling without taking over the entire style string definition core principle rendering should make formal signwriting visible without redefining what the encoded text is
