records.contentExcerpt: South Korea as a Current Institutional Case Study National dictionary publication, current institutional use, and why Korea matters This entry is the second major anchor of Sutton SignWriting Evidence and Adoption. South Korea should not be treated as only a vague international e...
records.searchText: south korea as a current institutional case study brief brief for south korea as a current institutional case study south korea shows signwriting functioning inside current public dictionary infrastructure connected to a national language institution national dictionary publication current institutional use and why korea matters series evidence and adoption south korea as a current institutional case study primary sutton signwriting evidence and adoption south korea as a current institutional case study brief brief research brief south korea as a current institutional case study south korea as a current institutional case study national dictionary publication current institutional use and why korea matters this entry is the second major anchor of sutton signwriting evidence and adoption south korea should not be treated as only a vague international example in signwriting conversations it matters for a narrower and very useful reason on february 6 2025 the national institute of korean language opened the korean sign language nuri dictionary with signwriting visible inside the public dictionary system that does not make south korea the strongest documented empirical center for signwriting brazil still carries that different claim much more strongly but it does make south korea a current institutional case study it shows that signwriting is already functioning inside a public language resource connected to a national language institution that is important because it changes the level of the conversation the question is no longer only whether signwriting could ever be used in serious public language work in south korea it already is why korea deserves separate treatment the korea case does a different job from the brazil case brazil shows documented institutional density educational spread and a strong research base south korea shows something narrower but very powerful present tense institutional visibility public dictionary infrastructure corpus based language work official publication context that combination makes korea especially useful when the conversation needs a current evidence point rather than a broader historical or educational map what happened on february 6 2025 the public launch record for the korean sign language nuri dictionary is tied to february 6 2025 the strongest public documentation currently available for this brief includes the dictionary site itself signwriting org s 2025 posting about the launch public news coverage previewing the february 6 launch from the national institute of korean language national institute of korean language pages listing the korean sign language nuri dictionary among its dictionary work the asia business daily article is dated february 5 2025 and updated february 6 2025 it is best read as an advance announcement and launch preview for the february 6 opening the launch materials describe the dictionary as based on korean sign language corpus data and designed to reflect actual sign language use the public description also states that deaf participants were involved throughout the compilation process at the time of opening the dictionary was described as containing approximately 1 000 entries the important point here is not only that a korean sign language dictionary exists it is that the dictionary includes signwriting in a public institutional setting that gives the korea case its present evidentiary value the date also matters because the case is new the korea brief should therefore be read as evidence of current official publication and institutional inclusion not yet as evidence of long term sustained educational adoption what the dictionary shows the public descriptions matter because they locate signwriting inside a real language resource rather than beside it as a symbolic gesture the relevant evidence is not every interface feature by itself the relevant evidence is that signwriting appears inside a corpus based public dictionary environment that also includes sign meanings korean equivalent expressions example usage sign form pictures sign language characters the public materials also describe search by korean and sign form sign language video menus and instructions and customizable display those features matter only insofar as they show that signwriting is part of a working lexicographic interface rather than a detached illustration this is the evidence claim signwriting is present inside actual public dictionary infrastructure connected to corpus based language work and national language institutional publication why the institutional shape matters the institutional shape is one of the strongest parts of the korea case this was not a private pilot or a one off classroom document it was a public dictionary launch tied to a national language institution that matters because public language institutions carry a different kind of weight they are not simply enthusiasts they are part of how language is described presented and made publicly available when signwriting appears there it gains a different kind of visibility and seriousness that is especially useful for infrastructure conversations because it shows that current public institutions can incorporate signwriting into real language tools evidence value south korea changes the evidentiary baseline in a useful way it shows that signwriting is not only preserved in older archives or used in isolated settings it can appear inside current official dictionary infrastructure that matters for several reasons it shows present tense public institutional inclusion it connects signwriting to corpus based language resource work it shows that signwriting can appear in a real dictionary interface it gives future documentation work a concrete institutional case to follow in other words korea helps move the evidence conversation from abstract possibility toward present institutional record limits of the claim the claim here is narrower than the brazil case this narrow claim does not claim that all of south korea writes korean sign language in signwriting that korean educational use has already been mapped in full that the korea case is larger than the brazil case that one dictionary settles every literacy or infrastructure question that a new 2025 dictionary launch already proves sustained long term use the point is different south korea already offers a current institutional case study that is enough to matter documentation needs the strongest evidence facing next steps are documentation tasks document the workflow behind the korean dictionary more clearly synthesize korean materials and presentations more accessibly in english track whether dictionary use maintenance and additions continue over time document how signwriting was produced reviewed and incorporated document deaf user editor and institutional perspectives where available distinguish dictionary publication from classroom adoption or ordinary writing practice south korea therefore deserves to be treated not as a passing international anecdote but as one of the clearest public evidence anchors that signwriting can function inside official language infrastructure for the broader empirical center see brazil as the empirical center of signwriting for stewardship consequences see sutton signwriting infrastructure and program after reading the country briefs see evidence boundaries and adoption limits for the claims that should not be overstated selected sources korean sign language nuri dictionary public dictionary site asia business daily 2025 february 5 national institute of korean language korean sign language nuri dictionary opens on the 6th signwriting org 2025 signwriting 2025 year end report south korean sign language dictionary national institute of korean language compiling and operating comprehensive dictionaries national institute of korean language history

records.title: Start Here Orientation
records.summary: Orientation for Start Here. Plain-language orientation to Sutton SignWriting, the Sutton SignWriting Foundations series, and the core public argument. What Sutton SignWriting is, why it matters, and where to go next
records.contentExcerpt: Start Here What Sutton SignWriting is, why it matters, and what to read first Sutton SignWriting is a writing system for signed languages. This work is about making signed language usable as real text, not only as video, gloss, or research transcription. That matters because sign...
records.searchText: start here orientation orientation for start here plain language orientation to sutton signwriting the sutton signwriting foundations series and the core public argument what sutton signwriting is why it matters and where to go next series foundations start here primary sutton signwriting foundations start here orientation orientation orientation start here start here what sutton signwriting is why it matters and what to read first sutton signwriting is a writing system for signed languages this work is about making signed language usable as real text not only as video gloss or research transcription that matters because signed languages are full human languages but they have often lacked one important layer durable readable and writable text within the language itself when a language has everyday writing people can do ordinary textual work with it they can jot something down revise a sentence compare two versions cite a passage search a collection annotate a lesson or build a reusable archive for many signed language communities those ordinary text practices have had to depend on tools that do not write the signed language directly video is powerful for recording signed language gloss is useful for cross language reference research transcription can help specialists analyze language but those tools do not do the same job as ordinary writing they do not give signers the same kind of direct written pathway that print gives many spoken languages or that braille gives blind readers signwriting matters because it offers one serious answer to that missing layer it can represent handshape movement facial marking and spatial structure in a form that can be read written revised taught cited and through compatible technical infrastructure searched and built into tools that does not make it a replacement for bilingual literacy video or community practice it makes it a written pathway for signed language itself two words recur across this series durable means that text can be revisited revised searched cited taught and reused infrastructure means the tools standards teaching institutions and stewardship that let writing persist the core idea the sutton signwriting foundations series makes two linked arguments first language is not sound human language can develop through different embodied pathways spoken language uses sound signed language uses vision and movement tactile language access matters too the point is not that modality is irrelevant the point is that no single sensory channel owns language second writing is not only a line many writing systems are line based but writing can also use space in other ways signwriting is important because it writes signed language through spatial composition rather than forcing it into a line first together those claims make room for signed language literacy on its own terms the first claim establishes that signed languages do not need sound in order to count as full language the second establishes that writing for signed languages does not need to imitate the line in order to count as full writing what to read there are three entries in this public sutton signwriting foundations series start here this orientation gives the simplest overview and names the main argument language without sound this essay explains why language is not reducible to speech and why signed languages need durable readable and writable pathways of their own the shape of writing this essay explains why writing is not inherently linear and why signwriting should be understood as plane based writing read those three in order if you are new to the project the larger platform the rest of the sutton signwriting platform carries the deeper specialist work sutton signwriting language and literacy research develops the research facing pathway model writing and representation in sutton signwriting expands the writing systems argument sutton signwriting evidence and adoption documents use feasibility and evidence boundaries sutton signwriting infrastructure and program explains stewardship and long term continuity formal signwriting documents the technical text model signed language writing critical review series evaluates literature standards proposals and recurring claims those series are not required before reading sutton signwriting foundations they are where the questions go next documentation path this foundations series gives the conceptual entry point the rest of the platform carries the detailed documentation language development literacy pathways and reading questions are developed in sutton signwriting language and literacy research public use evidence adoption limits and country level anchors are documented in sutton signwriting evidence and adoption plane based writing authored space and writing system theory are developed in writing and representation in sutton signwriting search encoding symbol identity validation layout rendering and software processing are documented in formal signwriting and unicode and signwriting continuity stewardship institutional support and program design are developed in sutton signwriting infrastructure and program why this matters durable text changes what people can do with language it can be revisited revised cited searched compared taught annotated processed and built into corpora and datasets for signed languages that is not only a technical question it is a literacy question a research question an educational question and an infrastructure question sutton signwriting foundations begins with the simplest claim signed languages are full human languages and they deserve durable written pathways of their own sutton signwriting is one serious answer to that need

records.title: Styling Signs Technical Paper
records.summary: Technical Paper for Styling Signs. The style string as optional light markup for customization and rich presentation. The style string as light markup for customization and rich presentation
records.contentExcerpt: Styling Signs The style string as light markup for customization and rich presentation The style string deserves its own technical context as a distinct presentation layer: optional light markup whole-sign customization symbol-level customization relation to rendering relation to...
records.searchText: styling signs technical paper technical paper for styling signs the style string as optional light markup for customization and rich presentation the style string as light markup for customization and rich presentation series formal signwriting styling signs primary formal signwriting styling signs technical paper technical paper technical paper styling signs styling signs the style string as light markup for customization and rich presentation the style string deserves its own technical context as a distinct presentation layer optional light markup whole sign customization symbol level customization relation to rendering relation to canonical text the style string is one of the easiest parts of the system to misplace taught inside encoding it muddies canonical text taught only inside rendering it stays under explained technical position the clearest position is fsw and swu are plain text encodings the style string is optional light markup that means the style string should not be treated as canonical sign text it is better understood as an attached presentation instruction layer a compact style example even a compact style example can carry several optional presentation controls this compact example shows several ideas at once styling is attached to the encoded item it can affect whole sign padding color and scale it can add whole sign class and id style hooks the underlying symbol key is still s10000 style string syntax the compact example can be read as at the paper level the useful syntax model is the initial target identifies what is being styled it may be a whole sign a symbol key or another supported output target depending on the renderer for whole sign styling the canonical sign text is the fsw or swu target before the first style string separator a parser that does not understand a later directive should preserve that target rather than discarding the sign after the target the style string has three hyphen delimited sections whole sign style for whole sign color padding background detail color and scale directives symbol level color for per symbol color overrides class and id for css class hooks and an optional id hook the middle and final sections may be empty in the compact example there is no symbol level color section so the boundary between the whole sign style section and the class and id section appears as the spaces in the explanatory forms are not serialized style string separators the serialized form is compact spaces are used only inside the class name list where they separate multiple css classes such as primary blinking the directives are compact presentation commands directive meaning c colorize the sign or target using default color behavior p10 apply padding of 10 units gblue set the background color to blue dred cyan set detail colors line red and fill cyan z1 1 scale output by 1 1 d01blue apply a symbol level color override in the middle section primary blinking add multiple css class hooks in the final section cursor add an id hook this is a compact renderer facing language rather than canonical sign grammar the exact accepted directive set belongs in implementation reference material but a publication entry should state enough syntax for readers to understand that the style string is structured parseable and separate from fsw or swu text whole sign customization the style string can describe appearance for an entire sign this includes whole sign padding whole sign scaling whole sign line and fill colors whole sign background color whole sign colorization behavior whole sign class and id hooks these controls matter because they make it easier to adapt signs for teaching display interfaces and publication contexts without changing the encoded sign text itself what whole sign styling can change whole sign size whole sign padding whole sign foreground and background colors whether whole sign output is colorized whole sign class and id hooks what it does not change the sign identity the symbol inventory the core encoded structure symbol level customization the style string can also target individual symbols within a sign that makes it useful for highlighting annotation teaching materials interface emphasis experimentation with visual presentation it is not merely a trivial suffix it is a lightweight system for rich customization for example symbol level directives can single out specific symbols for alternate coloring while leaving the rest of the sign unchanged classes and identifiers the style string can also provide class and id hooks for whole sign svg output that matters because it bridges sign data and web facing rendering workflows it allows richer interaction with css scripted behavior and interface level customization while still keeping those concerns outside the canonical text layer why the markup analogy helps the markup analogy is useful because it keeps two questions separate what the sign text is how the sign text is presented the analogy should not be pressed too far but it points in the right architectural direction styling is attached presentation instruction not the core content model relation to rendering the style string belongs near rendering because it affects appearance but it is better treated as a separate topic so rendering can focus on output models while styling can focus on customization semantics and interpretation core distinction separating styling from encoding and rendering keeps the central distinction clear canonical text remains stable rendering realizes that text visibly styling customizes the presentation of that rendering the style string can change how a sign is shown but it does not change what sign text is being encoded

records.contentExcerpt: Sutton SignWriting and Formal SignWriting Through the Framework A self-application of the review method to the writing system and its technical model This entry applies the review framework to SignWriting and Formal SignWriting themselves. One recurring problem in the literature...
records.searchText: sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework paper paper for sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework a self application distinguishing sutton signwriting as a human writing system from formal signwriting as its technical text model a self application of the review method to the writing system and its technical model series signed language writing critical review sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework primary signed language writing critical review series sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework paper framework application essay working paper sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework a self application of the review method to the writing system and its technical model this entry applies the review framework to signwriting and formal signwriting themselves one recurring problem in the literature is that signwriting and formal signwriting are often treated as though they were the same thing they are not signwriting is the writing system formal signwriting is the technical text model for signwriting it preserves encodes and processes written signs in software evaluating them together makes that distinction clearer and states more precisely what each one is executive summary the framework supports a strong but disciplined conclusion in this framework strong full path candidate is the strongest responsible positive classification for a system with a credible route across the full path it is not a claim of total closure signwriting is best understood as a plane based writing system whose primary written unit is the sign formal signwriting is best understood as the text model and encoding layer that preserves signwriting as structured text for storage search processing and rendering taken together they fare well under the framework because their layers can be kept distinct rather than collapsed they do not solve every problem in the broader field they do not by themselves establish universal adoption automatic literacy outcomes or complete scientific closure what they do establish is that signed language can be written as structured text and can be preserved in a usable technical model without reducing the writing to gloss video or symbol inventory alone that is already a substantial result baseline and open limits this paper is useful only if the baseline is stated plainly without turning into self certification signwriting and formal signwriting fare well under this framework because the framework is asking about durable signed writing structured text and infrastructure viability and those are precisely the questions on which the system is strongest that is not the same thing as saying the system is complete in every domain or settled in every respect important open limits remain including uneven adoption across regions and institutions incomplete large scale literacy evidence handwriting fluency and everyday uptake interoperability with mainstream software and standards so the point of the baseline is narrower it is to show that a coherent full path model already exists and can be described clearly enough that later work has to engage it at the right level that distinction also explains why the same evidence problem can be treated differently in different reviews when this entry names uneven adoption or incomplete outcome evidence as open limits it is not claiming those questions have already been settled when a reviewed source turns limited evidence into broad historical educational or comparative conclusions the problem becomes overextension the issue is not whether limits exist the issue is whether the argument admits them or builds past them declared dependencies matter here too the combined sutton signwriting and formal signwriting stack directly solves the human writing written unit spatial composition text model encoding search rendering tooling corpus and dictionary layers to a substantial degree some layers are externally supplied by use communities teachers dictionary contributors institutions public sites and existing publications other layers remain open or evidence limited broad literacy outcomes handwriting uptake institutional succession standards alignment and global adoption naming those dependencies prevents the self application from turning real infrastructure strength into a claim of total closure i layer identification the clearest starting point is the layer question signwriting belongs primarily at the writing system layer it is a public writing system for signed languages formal signwriting belongs primarily at the encoding and text model layer it is the technical model that preserves written signs and sign text for software use these are related but they are not interchangeable that distinction matters because much confusion in the literature comes from collapsing language writing notation encoding rendering signwriting and formal signwriting fare well here precisely because they allow these layers to be distinguished rather than merged this is a strong reason to evaluate them together without treating them as the same thing ii representation model signwriting is not well described as merely phonographic merely semantic or merely iconic its strongest orientation is structural it represents signed language as written composition on the plane where authored spatial relation is part of the text that does not mean form is irrelevant it means the writing system is not reducible to sequences of phonological like units formal signwriting continues this structural orientation at the technical layer its purpose is not to reinterpret the writing system its purpose is to preserve the structure of written signs in text form under the framework the combined model is strongest when stated this way signwriting is structural writing formal signwriting is structural text modeling iii structural unit analysis this is the decisive issue the primary written unit is not the isolated symbol it is the written sign that point has consequences all the way down symbols matter symbol inventories matter but they are not the primary written unit formal signwriting captures this more clearly by treating the sign as a structured element composed of an optional temporal sequence a required spatial signbox this is one of the main reasons the system fares well under the framework it does not stop at the symbol layer it preserves the written sign as the primary textual unit iv spatial vs linear organization signwriting is plane based writing space is not decoration added after the fact it is part of authorship that means linearization has to be treated carefully in formal signwriting the sequence exists but it does not replace the signbox the system therefore avoids a common failure in the literature flattening authored space into a line and then mistaking the line for the writing system itself this does not mean every question about spatial reading or processing is finished it means the model keeps the right thing primary v text model and encoding this is where the signwriting and formal signwriting combination is strongest signwriting alone is the writing system formal signwriting provides the technical layer needed to preserve that writing as text the core distinction remains writing is not encoding encoding is not rendering and rendering is not structure formal signwriting supports editable text searchable text segmentable text canonical forms within the current ecosystem software processing across storage search and rendering workflows this is one reason the combined system is not only conceptually interesting but infrastructurally real the writing system and the text model reinforce one another without collapsing into one layer vi human vs machine layers the framework also asks whether human writing and machine encoding are being kept distinct here again the signwriting and formal signwriting combination is comparatively strong at the human level writers select symbols place them in the signbox author spatial relations directly at the machine level formal signwriting stores and processes those authored structures fonts and renderers still matter but they do not define the structure itself that is a major difference from systems where rendering ends up deciding too much about the resulting written form here rendering remains important but it does not become the thing that defines the text vii empirical grounding a framework based self evaluation should still be disciplined here signwriting and formal signwriting are not only theoretical constructions they are grounded in real tools real dictionaries real corpora real published materials real educational and institutional use long running technical maintenance that is a major strength at the same time empirical maturity is uneven across different questions the system has stronger evidence for real writable text real technical processing real community and institutional use in some contexts it has weaker or still developing evidence for large scale reading research cross context literacy outcomes broader adoption beyond the current strongest centers that is not a failure of the model it is a reminder not to claim more than the current evidence supports viii claims vs evidence the strongest claims that do hold are signed language can be written as structured plane based text the written sign can be treated as the primary unit of text a technical model can preserve that structure for software use a real ecosystem of data tools fonts and publications can be built around that model the weaker or not yet settled claims are that any given educational or cognitive outcome follows automatically that adoption will generalize on its own that every open reading or processing question is already solved this is exactly where the framework is useful it keeps the claims disciplined even when the system itself fares well ix relationship to existing systems signwriting and formal signwriting do not enter the field as detached abstractions they are already the central existing system that later papers and proposals are often reacting to extending misdescribing or trying to replace that means the framework produces a different kind of question here not does this work acknowledge previous systems but does this system provide a coherent baseline that later work must address honestly the answer is yes that does not make the system immune from criticism it does mean later work has to engage its actual structure rather than a caricature framework judgment under the framework the strongest judgment is layer level first field judgment layer level judgment for sutton signwriting strong as a human writing system centered on written signs signboxes authored space written convention and reader writer practice layer level judgment for formal signwriting strong as a technical text model and encoding framework for preserving sutton signwriting as structured text full path judgment a strong full path candidate when sutton signwriting formal signwriting and the existing tooling corpus dictionary rendering and publication ecosystem are evaluated together main remaining limits evidence remains uneven for global adoption large scale literacy outcomes institutional succession and standards resolution the strongest point is not that the system is perfect it is that the layers can be kept clean writing system text model rendering software use that is exactly what many other accounts fail to do in that sense the framework does not simply vindicate the system it identifies more precisely where its real strength lies bottom line if the framework is good for evaluating others it should be good for evaluating signwriting and formal signwriting too applied that way it does not weaken the system it clarifies it the result is a clearer statement of what the system actually is a plane based writing system for signed language supported by a technical text model that preserves the written sign as structured text

questions and study design define the research agenda opened by signed language writing developmental pathways literacy layering reading questions and signed language research series language and literacy research overview primary sutton signwriting language and literacy research sutton signwriting language and literacy research overview overview series overview series overview overview sutton signwriting language and literacy research a research facing series this series develops the research questions opened by signed language writing it asks how accessible language pathways readable signed text and reusable written corpora can support stronger developmental literacy cognitive and language research it establishes a research agenda and study design surface it does not report completed outcome studies it is for psycholinguists cognitive scientists literacy researchers language researchers sign language researchers with developmental or experimental interests the central questions are how language development depends on accessible pathways for input and expression how literacy functions as a second developmental layer rather than primary language creation how the four pathway model should be stated tested and refined what signed language writing changes for development reading recognition syntax and research design what early studies materials corpora and reusable records.title: The Four Language Pathways Paper
records.summary: Paper for The Four Language Pathways. Language input, language output, literacy input, and literacy output create a research-facing model for signed-language writing. A developmental model of language, literacy, and signed-language writing
keywords: Sutton SignWriting Language and Literacy Research
records.contentExcerpt: The Four Language Pathways A developmental model of language, literacy, and signed-language writing This paper carries the opening model claim for Sutton SignWriting Language and Literacy Research: signed-language writing should be studied through distinct pathways for language i...
records.searchText: the four language pathways paper paper for the four language pathways language input language output literacy input and literacy output create a research facing model for signed language writing a developmental model of language literacy and signed language writing series language and literacy research the four language pathways primary sutton signwriting language and literacy research the four language pathways paper theoretical paper working paper the four language pathways the four language pathways a developmental model of language literacy and signed language writing this paper carries the opening model claim for sutton signwriting language and literacy research signed language writing should be studied through distinct pathways for language input language output literacy input and literacy output the model separates language development from literacy development it treats accessible input and expressive output as developmental necessities and it treats literacy as a durable symbolic layer that can be added after language begins for signed languages the research question is not only whether signing is language it is what changes when a signed language also has durable readable and writable text this paper turns the public foundations argument in language without sound into a research facing model it defines the four pathways explains why the distinctions matter and identifies the research space opened by signed language writing terminology note this paper uses developmental pathways in a broad sense it is about routes by which language and literacy become available to a learner and user language input language output literacy input literacy output it is not yet about the narrower reading pathway question of how written signs move from page to eye to perception to language interpretation during skilled reading when this paper says pathways without qualification it means developmental pathways unless the context says otherwise it also does not mean that each pathway maps to a discrete neural circuit here pathway is an access and development term it names how language or literacy becomes available for use not a completed neurocognitive model problem statement current discussion of signed language writing often still blurs three different things language as a developmental system literacy as a later symbolic layer modality as the route through which access happens that blur creates unnecessary confusion if language is treated as sound then signed language has to begin by defending its status as language if literacy is treated as equivalent to language then early language access and later symbolic access are hard to separate if modality is treated as destiny then questions about accessibility deprivation literacy and writing are reduced to preference for one channel rather than analyzed as developmental pathway questions the four pathway model separates those issues while preserving the importance of modality its claim is narrower what matters developmentally is whether accessible pathways exist for language input language output literacy input and literacy output that shift allows a clearer account of what comes first what comes second and what signed language writing changes this paper also distinguishes literacy for deaf signers from literacy within signed language deaf signers may and often must develop literacy in surrounding written languages such as portuguese english korean or other national and regional written languages that remains essential the present model asks a different question what changes when the signed language itself also has durable readable and writable text relation to existing research input output language and literacy are not new ideas by themselves language acquisition research has long shown that timing and accessibility of language input matter sign language research has shown that signed languages are full natural languages and that delayed first language access can have lasting consequences reading research has long distinguished language comprehension decoding or recognition and broader comprehension processes this paper uses those established findings for a narrower purpose it asks how the pieces should be reorganized when the language in question is signed and when the proposed literacy layer is not print for a surrounding spoken language but durable written signed language that makes the model different from several familiar frameworks the simple view of reading is useful for separating linguistic comprehension from text recognition but it was built around print reading questions and does not by itself ask whether a signed language has direct literacy input and output pathways of its own dual route and word recognition models help describe how printed words may be recognized or read aloud but they do not define the developmental relationship among signed language access signed language literacy and durable signed text language deprivation and critical period research help explain why early accessible language matters but they do not by themselves describe what changes when direct signed language literacy becomes available later the model s contribution is therefore not a replacement for those frameworks it is a sorting model for the signed language writing problem separate developmental language pathways from durable literacy pathways then ask which pathways actually exist for the learner and the community more formally the four pathway model is a research organizing model rather than a replacement for acquisition theory reading theory or neurocognitive models of language its purpose is to sort the signed language writing problem into distinct access conditions language input language output literacy input literacy output the model is useful if it helps researchers state which pathway is present constrained delayed or missing and what kind of evidence would be needed to study that condition language comes first the first major developmental issue is not reading it is language a child develops language when linguistic input is accessible socially meaningful and repeatedly available and when the child also has usable expressive routes back out into interaction this point is easy to lose when literacy is culturally overvalued or when speech is treated as the default model of language but developmentally language and literacy are not coequal starting points language is primary literacy builds later on top of it this is true whether the early language route is auditory and spoken visual and signed tactile in forms of tactile language access the crucial issue is not which modality is socially dominant the crucial issue is whether the learner has real access to language that requires distinguishing two questions that are often collapsed what language is present in the environment what language is actually accessible to the learner those are not the same thing a language can be socially available without being stably accessible that difference matters because the developmental costs of limited or unstable access emerge before literacy can plausibly compensate literacy comes second literacy should be treated as a second developmental layer it does not create language from nothing it adds durable symbolic routes to an already linguistic system those routes matter because they allow language to be revisited revised compared indexed searched shared across time and distance this is why literacy is more than storage it changes what language users can do with language it also changes what teachers researchers and institutions can do with language for the purposes of this paper the key point is that literacy adds durable symbolic input and output that addition should not be confused with early language formation itself language first literacy second the second depends on the first more than the first depends on the second the four pathways the model proposed here distinguishes four pathways language input pathways how language reaches the learner as language language output pathways how the learner expresses language back into interaction literacy input pathways how language is received as durable symbolic text literacy output pathways how language is authored as durable symbolic text the point of the model is not to produce a neat quadrant for its own sake the point is to clarify that early language access and later literacy access are related but not identical developmental conditions language input language input pathways are primary because they nourish the formation of a language system here again pathway is being used in the access and development sense defined above not as a claim that each pathway is a discrete neural circuit examples include auditory speech for many hearing children visual signed language for many deaf children with early sign access tactile language access in deafblind contexts these routes are not interchangeable in practice but they do the same kind of developmental work they make language available to the learner as language language output language output pathways matter because language development is not only receptive children need ways to express language into the world test it in interaction and participate socially in it examples include speaking signing tactile expressive systems in deafblind communication output matters because language grows through use not only through passive reception literacy input literacy input pathways are later arising durable symbolic routes examples include print reading braille reading potentially signwriting reading what unites them is not modality but durability and symbolic structure literacy output literacy output pathways allow the learner to author durable symbolic language examples include handwriting typing braille writing potentially signwriting composition these pathways complete the literacy layer by making written language not only readable but writable model statement and testable expectations the model can be stated compactly language development depends first on accessible language input and usable language output literacy adds later durable symbolic input and output pathways that can amplify preserve and reorganize language use once language exists for signed languages the key research consequence is direct signed language writing adds literacy input and literacy output pathways inside the signed language rather than only through video gloss transcription or a surrounding spoken language print system several expectations follow early language access should matter more than sensory channel learners with early accessible signed language should be expected to build linguistic foundations even without auditory speech access learners with delayed or unstable first language access should face constrained developmental opportunity regardless of whether later literacy instruction is strong direct signed language literacy should create different text use affordances if signwriting functions as literacy input and literacy output readers and writers should be able to use it for tasks that video and gloss do not support as directly rereading revision annotation comparison citation corpus search classroom reuse literacy output should matter not only literacy input a learner who can read signed text but cannot easily author it has only part of the literacy layer writing typing editing and revising should therefore be studied as part of the model rather than treated as secondary after reading the model should be revised if durable signed text does not change use the model would be weakened if direct signed language text did not produce distinctive affordances over video gloss or surrounding language print in tasks where durability should matter those tasks include annotation revision search comparison reuse citation classroom return and corpus study especially when compared with video only or gloss only conditions it would also need revision if reader experience classroom use corpus work and recognition studies failed to show learnable regularities in signed text that is why this paper is a model paper not a proof that every empirical question is settled pathway interactions the four pathways should not be imagined as sealed compartments they interact language input and language output form the core developmental exchange through which language is built and exercised literacy input and literacy output later intersect with that system by making language durable and symbolically manipulable that means the model is developmental not merely taxonomic it is meant to explain order and dependence language pathways are foundational literacy pathways are later augmentations literacy pathways can amplify language use once language exists pathway constraints and developmental cost this model becomes useful because it clarifies what it means for pathways to be constrained the most careful statement is not that every constrained pathway produces the same outcome or that the brain simply stagnates in a literal sense the more defensible claim is that reduced delayed unstable or inaccessible pathways constrain developmental opportunity that matters especially for the language layer if language input is delayed or partial then later literacy has less stable structure to build on if expressive output is constrained then the learner has less opportunity to participate in reciprocal language use if literacy pathways are missing then language may still be fully linguistic but it lacks durable symbolic routes that support revision comparison textual accumulation and broader forms of educational and research reuse different pathway constraints have different effects the model s central claim is that pathway configuration matters that access matters and that developmental timing matters signed language writing as a new literacy condition this is where signed language writing becomes more than an abstract add on if a signed language gains durable readable and writable text of its own then the pathway landscape changes what becomes possible is not only better notation or better archiving a new literacy condition becomes available literacy input within the signed language itself literacy output within the signed language itself this matters because video surrounding spoken language literacy and research transcription systems do not all do the same job video preserves performance but it is not the same as durable text literacy in a surrounding spoken language can be powerful and necessary but it is not the same as native signed language literacy research transcription systems can be analytically useful but they are not always designed for ordinary readable and writable use the question then is not whether signed people can use video or bilingual literacy the question is what changes when signed language itself has durable symbolic pathways that is the condition under which signwriting becomes scientifically meaningful what this model establishes the model claims language development and literacy development should be distinguished accessible pathways matter more than loyalty to one sensory channel literacy is a second developmental layer signed language writing changes the literacy landscape in principle that changed landscape opens real research questions the model is a research framework not an outcome claim its value is that it separates questions that are often collapsed early language access later literacy input output signed language writing reading syntax and educational outcomes those distinctions make future empirical work easier to design and easier to interpret the usefulness of the model lies partly in its ability to define a research space without pretending that the research space has already been exhausted research questions that follow once the four pathways are separated a more disciplined research program becomes possible development and access what kinds of early pathway configurations best support strong language development how should language access and literacy access be distinguished in educational design what are the consequences of direct signed language literacy pathways becoming available later than language pathways reading and recognition what does reading mean in a plane based signed writing system how do readers resolve structure similarity and spatial relation what kinds of near neighbor competition emerge in signed text syntax and spatial reference how are spatial relations anaphora and structured reference carried into written form what does syntactic organization look like when writing is not modeled only on a line education and institutional use how do learners use written signed language in real educational settings what changes when text rather than video alone becomes part of classroom practice corpora and reusable infrastructure what texts should be collected what annotation and search layers are needed how can studies become cumulative rather than isolated these questions are not all answered here they are made clearer here why this paper belongs after language without sound language without sound was right to insist that language is not reducible to sound but once that conceptual ground is cleared the next task is not to repeat the claim louder the next task is to model the developmental and literacy consequences more explicitly that is what this paper is for it should therefore be read as a second move not a replacement for the foundations essay not a compressed infrastructure pitch but the first real anchor of the research facing series conclusion the four pathway model is a way of clarifying the developmental order of the problem language comes first literacy comes second both depend on accessible pathways but they do not depend on the same kinds of pathways in the same way once that distinction is made signed language writing becomes easier to understand it is not merely a novel notation it is the possibility of adding durable literacy pathways to a language that has too often been forced to rely on performance video or surrounding language text for functions that durable native text could support more directly that does not settle the science it gives the science a clearer model from which to begin from here the strongest next move is research program for literacy without sound which carries the model into study design dataset priorities and reusable research infrastructure selected references campbell r macsweeney m waters d 2008 sign language and the brain a review journal of deaf studies and deaf education 13 1 3 20 emmorey k 2002 language cognition and the brain insights from sign language research lawrence erlbaum associates gough p b tunmer w e 1986 decoding reading and reading disability remedial and special education 7 1 6 10 hoover w a gough p b 1990 the simple view of reading reading and writing 2 127 160 macsweeney m woll b campbell r mcguire p k david a s williams s c r suckling j calvert g a brammer m j 2002 neural systems underlying british sign language and audio visual english processing in native users brain 125 7 1583 1593 mayberry r i lock e kazmi h 2002 linguistic ability and early language exposure nature 417 38 newport e l 1990 maturational constraints on language learning cognitive science 14 1 11 28 p nicaud s klein d zatorre r j chen j k witcher p hyde k mayberry r i 2013 structural brain changes linked to delayed first language acquisition in congenitally deaf individuals neuroimage 66 42 49 petitto l a langdon c stone a andriola d kartheiser g cochran c 2016 visual sign phonology insights into human reading and language from a natural soundless phonology wires cognitive science 7 6 366 381

records.title: The Shape of a Sign Technical Paper
records.summary: Technical Paper for The Shape of a Sign. Symbols, coordinates, centering, and signbox structure define the spatial layer of a Formal SignWriting word. Symbols, coordinates, centering, and the signbox
records.contentExcerpt: The Shape of a Sign Symbols, coordinates, centering, and the signbox The signbox is where Formal SignWriting becomes visibly spatial and technically precise. It is also the clearest public handoff from the writing-systems series into the formal technical model. Its core claim is...
records.searchText: the shape of a sign technical paper technical paper for the shape of a sign symbols coordinates centering and signbox structure define the spatial layer of a formal signwriting word symbols coordinates centering and the signbox series formal signwriting the shape of a sign primary formal signwriting the shape of a sign technical paper technical paper technical paper the shape of a sign the shape of a sign symbols coordinates centering and the signbox the signbox is where formal signwriting becomes visibly spatial and technically precise it is also the clearest public handoff from the writing systems series into the formal technical model its core claim is simple the signbox is a spatial arrangement carried in text working understanding a reader should be able to explain what a signbox is why symbols belong to technical classes how coordinates make spatial writing usable in software why normalization matters minimal vocabulary the spatial layer depends on a small vocabulary term meaning signbox the composition preserving part of the sign symbol class a technically distinct range of symbols coordinate an encoded spatial placement using the top left of the symbol image tight bounding box the top left and bottom right extent of a symbol or sign normalization preprocessing that computes tight bounding boxes centers the sign and determines maximum coordinates if these terms stay clear the spatial side of the model stays clear the signbox is a spatial arrangement formal signwriting is not only a string format the signbox is where writing symbols become an arranged written object it matters because the system has to preserve visible composition while still working as text in software symbols formal signwriting depends on defined symbol classes and symbol ranges this is not only a matter of cataloging different symbol classes play different technical roles in writing centering searching and rendering at a glance class fsw range why it matters writing symbols s100 to s37e core sign composition detailed location s37f to s386 sequence and location handling punctuation s387 to s38b sentence and text structure these ranges are not merely taxonomic they help determine which symbols belong inside ordinary sign composition which symbols support more detailed location handling which symbols behave as punctuation in larger passages of sign text later layers depend on those distinctions search rendering centering and filtering all become easier to define when symbol classes are explicit implementations often expose these distinctions through kinds categories groups and named ranges two named ranges matter especially for centering hcenter for horizontal centering vcenter for vertical centering coordinates the signbox uses coordinates to preserve spatial relation these coordinates are not incidental metadata they are part of how the written form becomes usable in software the normalized sign center used by later layout is 500x500 that common center lets an authored signbox keep its internal spatial composition while still aligning predictably inside passages lanes and rendered output for example the signbox portion of this sign contains m525x535 as the signbox marker and signbox coordinate four writing symbols each with its own coordinate that is how visible arrangement becomes text software can work with at a finer level each placed symbol follows the same pattern this can be read as s2e748 the symbol key 483x510 the top left coordinate of that symbol image inside the signbox the repeated pairing of symbol key plus coordinate is what lets the signbox function as a structured spatial object rather than a loose visual impression bounding boxes and maximum coordinates bounding box logic matters because written signs do not encode symbol size directly formal signwriting therefore needs a clear account of symbol bounding boxes sign bounding boxes maximum coordinate calculations those calculations support layout sizing and downstream rendering behavior the basic logic is each symbol has its own measured width and height each placed symbol starts with a symbol key and a top left coordinate preprocessing temporarily expands each symbol into a tight bounding box with top left and bottom right coordinates the sign bounding box is derived from the extreme left right top and bottom extents across all placed symbols maximum coordinate calculations make the full spatial footprint explicit that is why a sign can be laid out centered printed and rendered without abandoning its textual form in other words the sign remains text the text carries its spatial arrangement and that arrangement can be computed consistently centering and normalization centering is one of the most important technical moves in the system it simplifies layout and supports more consistent spatial handling across tools it also improves comparison between implementations stable sign display in reusable interfaces downstream search and rendering workflows practical interchange across software generations the centering logic should be stated more concretely horizontal centering first looks for hcenter symbols vertical centering first looks for vcenter symbols if those ranges are absent centering falls back to all placed symbols the midpoint is calculated from the selected minimum and maximum extents all symbol coordinates are shifted together the resulting maximum x and y values become part of the preprocessing result the temporary bottom right coordinates are then discarded leaving normalized sign text the important point is that normalization requires symbol size knowledge that is not stored directly in the written sign the sign remains text but its spatial layout has to be computed during preprocessing the conceptual point is then clear the sign must be normalized strongly enough to support stable technical use core point without this spatial layer formal signwriting risks being misunderstood as only a string format with it the model becomes visibly coherent and usable in software related platform routes sign text layout explains how centered signboxes become vertical and horizontal passages rendering formal signwriting explains how that layout becomes visible output

records.title: The Shape of Writing Paper
records.summary: Paper for The Shape of Writing. Writing is not inherently linear, and SignWriting is a strong case of plane-based writing. Why signed writing does not need to fit into a line
records.contentExcerpt: The Shape of Writing Why signed writing does not need to fit into a line Most people think writing is a line. They think this because most modern literacy is taught through line-based text. Letters proceed one after another. Words proceed one after another. Sentences proceed one...
records.searchText: the shape of writing paper paper for the shape of writing writing is not inherently linear and signwriting is a strong case of plane based writing why signed writing does not need to fit into a line series foundations the shape of writing primary sutton signwriting foundations the shape of writing paper theoretical paper working paper the shape of writing the shape of writing why signed writing does not need to fit into a line most people think writing is a line they think this because most modern literacy is taught through line based text letters proceed one after another words proceed one after another sentences proceed one after another that familiarity is real it is not the definition of writing writing can use space in different ways that matters for how writing is read written edited encoded searched and shared it also matters for how signwriting should be understood the core claim is simple writing is not inherently linear once that is accepted a second claim becomes easier to see signed language does not need to be forced into a line in order to be written writing and space the clearest way to see this is to ask how writing uses space this essay uses a working model point line and plane these are not sealed boxes they are a way to notice which spatial organization is doing the main work in a particular kind of writing three broad patterns matter point structure is concentrated into a bounded written unit line meaning unfolds mainly through ordered sequence plane meaning depends on spatial composition across two dimensions point line and plane are broad patterns for how writing organizes language in space across this platform plane based writing names writing in which two dimensional spatial composition is part of authorship many writing systems combine these patterns but the distinction matters because it loosens a mistaken assumption writing does not come in only one shape point point like writing compresses structure into a bounded unit the unit may still contain internal parts those parts may matter for lookup learning analysis or history but readers still approach the written form as an integrated whole chinese characters are a useful example at the unit level that does not mean chinese writing is non sequential at every level characters still occur in strings phrases and sentences the narrower point is this a written unit can function as a whole and still be decomposed for sorting indexing and software handling this matters because it separates two ideas that are often confused integrated writing does not mean unanalyzable writing decomposition for technical purposes does not make the writing fundamentally linear line line based writing is the familiar modern case meaning unfolds through sequence order matters adjacency matters parsing depends heavily on progression alphabetic writing abjads abugidas and many other systems are line dominant in this sense in these systems the writer mainly provides a sequence typography shaping and layout often handle much of the final rendering ligatures combining marks stacked signs and cursive joining may create visual complexity but the dominant model remains writing is authored as a sequence and rendered as a line that contrast matters because it makes the next step easier to see in line based systems the writer mainly provides a sequence in signwriting the writer composes spatial relations as part of the writing itself plane plane based writing uses two dimensional space directly position matters relation matters grouping matters sometimes simultaneity matters the writer does not merely choose symbols in order the writer composes them spatially this is where signwriting becomes especially important a written sign is not only a list of handshapes movements contacts orientations facial markers and locations it is a spatial composition in which those relations are part of the writing itself that is why the clearest statement is plane writing makes spatial relation part of authorship this does not mean every visual notation counts as a writing system it does mean that signwriting can be understood as a genuine case of writing that works through the plane rather than through a line alone signwriting as plane based writing once the default assumption of linearity is removed signwriting becomes easier to see clearly it can be understood as a writable way of composing signed language in space the written sign is not authored mainly as a sequence and then finished by layout it is authored through symbol choice and spatial relation from the start that matters because signed language is already spatial in structure and expression if a writing system for signed language exists it does not need to imitate the shape of spoken print in order to be real it needs to be adequate to the language it writes that is what makes plane based writing so important here the alphabet is part of the writing plane based writing is not free placement alone it also depends on a stable symbol inventory for modern sutton signwriting that shared inventory is iswa 2010 the international signwriting alphabet 2010 symbol set here alphabet means the stable signwriting symbol inventory it does not mean a small linear alphabet modeled on spoken language print this matters because the alphabet is the reference layer behind writing editors fonts websites datasets and encoding handshape palm facing fill rotation and related contrasts belong to symbol identity those distinctions are part of the writing system they are not mere display preferences if tools fonts or websites silently redefine them the result is not harmless variation the result is fractured technical infrastructure this is why the alphabet belongs inside the argument about the shape of writing the plane depends on a stable alphabet not only on free placement human writing and machine encoding at this point one distinction becomes essential human writing and machine encoding are not the same layer human writing this concerns what writers compose what readers perceive which spatial relations matter which symbols belong to the writing itself for signwriting this layer is planar machine encoding this concerns storage transmission search sorting parsing computation a writing system can be plane based for readers and writers while still needing a serialized form for software that is not a contradiction it is an encoding layer design question the important point is serialization for software does not prove that the writing was really linear all along why this matters for signed language signed languages are full human languages the pathway model from sutton signwriting foundations language without sound applies here language pathways are complete without writing while literacy adds durable symbolic pathways that change what can be read written revised and shared but they have often lacked equally durable written pathways instead they have often been pushed toward video gloss researcher facing transcription line based tools that do not match the spatial shape of the language those tools are useful they are not the same thing as writable text video preserves performance gloss supports cross language reference transcription supports analysis but none of them automatically create everyday readable and writable text within the language itself that is the infrastructure gap signed languages have full linguistic structure but they have often lacked equally durable writable pathways that gap affects literacy editing comparison lexicons corpora annotation search datasets long term infrastructure here infrastructure gap includes both technical layers such as text models fonts editors corpora and software and human layers such as continuity teaching adoption stewardship and publication practice comparative cases the differences become easier to see through a few simple comparisons hearing child plus print literacy a hearing child usually develops spoken language first and then line based literacy through print the written pathway is sequential text deaf child plus signwriting a deaf child may develop signed language first and then potentially gain literacy through a writable plane based system the written pathway is spatial text spatial specialist notation mathematics music chemistry and other specialist notations already show that literate readers can work with systems in which placement grouping and layout carry meaning these are not identical to full natural language writing but they show that literacy does not have to be purely linear video or transcription of sign video and transcription are valuable they preserve language for documentation and research but they do not by themselves provide the same kind of everyday readable and writable text that a writing system provides what follows once this is understood if signwriting is understood as plane based writing then several consequences follow if spatial relation is part of authorship technical systems must preserve not only symbol identity but also authored placement encoding layout search rendering and font compatibility are therefore part of preserving the writing not peripheral display details it needs a stable alphabet stable symbol identity compatible tools processable encoding shared editors fonts and websites corpora datasets and research support it also needs an encoding model that preserves authored composition rather than trying to replace it with derived rendering in other words the question is no longer whether signed language could be written the question becomes what infrastructure is required once signed language has writable text of its own that is why this argument matters it is not only a theory of writing it is also a practical claim about literacy tools compatibility and research support conclusion writing does not come in only one shape some writing compresses structure into bounded units some unfolds through sequence some composes meaning in space signwriting belongs in that third case strongly enough to matter once that is seen signed language no longer has to be treated as if writing for it must imitate the line in order to count readable and writable plane based text becomes thinkable and signwriting shows that it is already possible in practice visible composition can remain with the writer and processable encoding can support that writing without redefining it and once that becomes thinkable a whole new layer of literacy tooling compatibility datasets and research becomes possible
